source: Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (artificialanalysis.ai) - reverse-engineered from methodology page, evaluations pages, changelog, and 2025-2026 press coverage
summary: Artificial Analysis (AA) runs the de facto industry composite leaderboard. The CURRENT version is Intelligence Index v4.0 (sub-versioned to v4.0.4; launched ~Dec 2025), NOT v2/v3 as the brief guessed. v4.0 is a deliberate de-saturation overhaul: top models now peak at ~50 (older snapshot: GPT-5.2 xhigh 50, Opus 4.5 49, Gemini 3 Pro 48) DOWN from 73 in v3.0, and on the live site as of Jun 2026 the frontier has climbed to ~60-65 (Claude Fable 5 ~65, Opus 4.8 ~61, GPT-5.5 xhigh ~60). v4.0 retired three saturated, marketing-overused benches (MMLU-Pro, AIME 2025, LiveCodeBench - now kept only as standalone evals) and replaced them with harder/real-world ones. COMPOSITION (10 evals in 4 equal-weight categories of 25% each): AGENTS = GDPval-AA (16.7%) + tau2-Bench Telecom (8.3%); CODING = Terminal-Bench Hard (16.7%) + SciCode (8.3%); GENERAL = AA-Omniscience (12.5%) + AA-LCR (6.25%) + IFBench (6.25%); SCIENTIFIC REASONING = Humanity's Last Exam/HLE (12.5%) + GPQA Diamond (6.25%) + CritPt (6.25%). AGGREGATION: weighted average of per-eval normalized scores; most evals are pass@1 accuracy as a fraction, but GDPval-AA is an ELO pairwise-judge score normalized via clamp((Elo-500)/2000) and frozen at model-add time (GPT-5.1 non-reasoning anchored to Elo 1000), and AA-Omniscience contributes 50% accuracy + 50% (1 - hallucination rate). Index is on a ~0-100 scale; 95% CI stated as <±1% (from >10 repeats on select models). REASONING vs NON-REASONING: non-reasoning temp=0 / 16,384 max output tokens; reasoning temp=0.6 / max output tokens = creator-disclosed budget (per-model custom); repeats vary per eval (1 to 5). COST/SPEED: price = USD per 1M tokens blended at a default 7:2:1 cache:input:output ratio; they also publish 'cost to run the full Index'; speed = output tokens/sec (after first token), Time to First Token, and Time to First Answer Token (after thinking). PRESENTATION: intelligence-vs-price scatter (the signature quadrant chart), intelligence-vs-output-speed, intelligence-vs-tokens-used, intelligence-over-time trend, filterable leaderboard (29 of 529 models selectable, open-vs-proprietary filter), per-model and per-provider pages, and a per-eval breakdown table.
key_takeaways: CURRENT VERSION = Intelligence Index v4.0 (granular v4.0.4), launched ~Dec 2025. The brief's guess of v2/v3 is stale. v3.0 was the prior version (10 evals: MMLU-Pro, GPQA Diamond, HLE, LiveCodeBench, SciCode, AIME 2025, IFBench, AA-LCR, Terminal-Bench Hard, tau2-Bench Telecom).

v4.0 was EXPLICITLY a de-saturation move - the single most relevant fact for us. AA themselves dropped MMLU-Pro, AIME 2025 and LiveCodeBench because frontier models had compressed them; top score fell from 73 (v3.0) to 50 (v4.0 launch). This is the same saturation failure our v0 suite has. They solved it by swapping in harder real-world/agentic evals, not by reweighting.

COMPOSITION v4.0 = 4 categories at 25% each, 10 evals with explicit sub-weights: GDPval-AA 16.7% + tau2-Bench Telecom 8.3% (Agents); Terminal-Bench Hard 16.7% + SciCode 8.3% (Coding); AA-Omniscience 12.5% + AA-LCR 6.25% + IFBench 6.25% (General); HLE 12.5% + GPQA Diamond 6.25% + CritPt 6.25% (Scientific Reasoning).

AGGREGATION = weighted average of normalized per-eval scores on ~0-100 scale. Most evals = pass@1 accuracy fraction. Two special cases: GDPval-AA uses ELO from pairwise LLM-judging, normalized clamp((Elo-500)/2000) and frozen at add-time; AA-Omniscience = 50% accuracy + 50% (1 - hallucination rate). 95% CI stated <±1% from >10 repeats.

REASONING HANDLING is concrete and copyable: non-reasoning models temp=0, 16,384 max output tokens; reasoning models temp=0.6, max-output = creator-disclosed thinking budget (per-model custom). Repeats per eval are tuned to variance: HLE/GDPval/AA-Omniscience=1, tau2/Terminal-Bench/SciCode/AA-LCR=3, IFBench/GPQA/CritPt=5.

AGENTIC/CODING EVALS NEED HEAVY HARNESSES - confirms our 'known trap'. GDPval-AA needs an E2B sandbox + Web Fetch/Search/Shell tools (100-turn); Terminal-Bench Hard needs the Terminus 2 harness (100 episodes, 1M input tokens/repeat); tau2-Bench needs a dual-control simulated-user agent. These are NOT runnable by a local user on an OpenAI-compatible endpoint without infra.

CONTAMINATION RESISTANCE via public/private split: AA-Omniscience publishes only 600 of 6,000 questions (10%) on HuggingFace; the other 5,400 are a private hold-out. AA-LCR and GDPval-AA are AA-proprietary. This private-holdout-with-small-public-sample pattern is the contamination mitigation - directly applicable to our sentinel-set design.

DISCRIMINATION FOR SMALL MODELS IS STILL LIMITED, even in v4.0. Best open-weight models sit only 3-6 pts behind frontier (Kimi K2.6 / MiMo V2.5 Pro ~54, DeepSeek V4 Pro ~52 vs GPT-5.5 xhigh ~60). The index discriminates at the FRONTIER but compresses near the top; it is built for big models, so it does NOT automatically solve our small-local-model spread problem.

PRESENTATION blueprint to emulate: the signature is the intelligence-vs-price scatter (log-x price, y=index, 'most attractive quadrant' = top-left). Plus intelligence-vs-output-speed, intelligence-vs-tokens-consumed (efficiency), intelligence-over-time, a filterable table with open-vs-proprietary toggle, and per-model/per-provider drill-downs with a per-eval breakdown and 'cost to run the suite'.

COST/SPEED METRICS we should mirror: blended USD/1M-tokens at a stated 7:2:1 cache:input:output ratio, plus the literal dollar cost to run the whole suite per model; speed = output tokens/sec measured after first token, Time-to-First-Token, and Time-to-First-Answer-Token (excludes thinking time for reasoning models).
lessons_for_us: Saturation is the enemy and reweighting won't fix it - AA's own answer was to REPLACE saturated benches with harder ones, not rebalance. Our rebuild should likewise retire saturated axes (our MMLU-Pro subset behaves like their dropped MMLU-Pro) and add genuinely hard, frontier-discriminating content. Borrow GPQA Diamond, HLE, IFBench, SciCode as servable candidates.

Don't blindly adopt AA's agentic/coding evals - they require sandboxes/harnesses (E2B, Terminus 2, dual-control sims) that violate our local-runnable constraint. For local-bench, prefer the static-scored members of AA's set (GPQA Diamond, HLE text-only, IFBench, SciCode subproblems, AA-Omniscience public 600) and treat true agentic eval as a separate, optional, harness-gated tier.

Copy AA's reasoning-vs-non-reasoning protocol verbatim as a defensible default: temp 0 / 16,384 tokens for non-reasoning, temp 0.6 / creator-disclosed max-tokens for reasoning, with per-eval repeat counts tuned to variance (1-5). This solves our 'how to fairly run thinking budgets' question with an industry-standard precedent we can cite.

Adopt the public-sample + private-holdout pattern (AA-Omniscience ships 10% public, 90% private) as our contamination mitigation. It lets us show the community the format without leaking the scored set - exactly what our generated-math sentinel split was reaching for.

Note the key DIVERGENCE in our use cases: AA's index compresses to ~6 pts between best-open and frontier and is built to separate billion-dollar frontier labs. WE must separate a 9B from a 32B from frontier. So we likely need harder discrimination at the LOW end too (e.g. retain some mid-difficulty items the frontier aces but small models fail), not just frontier-busting items - a pure AA clone would re-saturate at our small-model floor.

Steal the presentation IA wholesale: intelligence-vs-price quadrant scatter as the hero chart, plus speed and token-efficiency scatters, open-vs-proprietary filter, per-model breakdown table, and a literal 'cost to run the suite' figure. Our local-bench differentiator = same charts but x-axis = consumer hardware / VRAM / quant, and we already log cost+timing per run.

Use a weighted-category structure (4 buckets x 25%) rather than a flat mean of axes - it prevents one easy axis from dominating (the exact failure where our Gemini 'beat' Opus by acing easy axes). Categories force balance across knowledge/reasoning/coding/agentic.

Normalize per-eval before aggregating and keep scores on a 0-100 composite, but beware mixing metric types (accuracy fractions vs ELO vs hallucination-penalized index) - AA does this with explicit per-eval transforms (clamp((Elo-500)/2000); 0.5*acc + 0.5*(1-halluc)). If we add a hallucination axis, copy the +1/-1/0 Omniscience scoring to reward calibrated abstention.
benchmarks_used: GDPval-AA (220 tasks, real-world knowledge work, ELO pairwise-judge, E2B sandbox + web/shell tools, Agents 16.7%)
tau2-Bench Telecom (114 tasks, dual-control agentic, Agents 8.3%)
Terminal-Bench Hard (44 tasks, 2025, Terminus 2 harness, Coding 16.7%)
SciCode (288 subproblems, code generation, Coding 8.3%)
AA-Omniscience (6,000 Qs, 42 topics/6 domains, knowledge+hallucination, 600 public/5,400 private, General 12.5%)
AA-LCR (100 Qs, long-context reasoning, proprietary, General 6.25%)
IFBench (294 Qs, instruction following, General 6.25%)
Humanity's Last Exam / HLE (2,158 text-only Qs, Scientific Reasoning 12.5%)
GPQA Diamond (198 Qs, 2024/2025 revision, Scientific Reasoning 6.25%)
CritPt (70 challenges, physics research reasoning, Scientific Reasoning 6.25%)
DROPPED in v3.0->v4.0 for saturation, now standalone: MMLU-Pro, AIME 2025, LiveCodeBench
confidence_interval_treatment: AA states a 95% confidence interval for the overall Intelligence Index of less than +/-1%, derived from experiments with more than 10 repeats on certain models across all v4.0 evaluation datasets. Individual evaluations can have wider CIs than the composite. GDPval-AA CIs are computed via bootstrap resampling - refitting the ELO model 1,000 times on resampled match data. Per-question repeats are tuned to each eval's variance to control noise: HLE / GDPval-AA / AA-Omniscience = 1 repeat; tau2-Bench / Terminal-Bench Hard / SciCode / AA-LCR = 3 repeats; IFBench / GPQA Diamond / CritPt = 5 repeats. Net: the composite is reported as a point score (~0-100) with the understanding that sub-eval noise is averaged out by the weighting and repeat scheme; they do not appear to render visible error bars on the main scatter, treating <±1% as negligible for ranking.
handles_saturation_how: Saturation is the explicit reason v4.0 exists. By v3.0 the top models had compressed the leaderboard (peak ~73) and the staple benches (MMLU-Pro, AIME 2025, LiveCodeBench) no longer separated frontier models - and were also heavily gamed in vendor marketing. AA's remedy was REPLACEMENT, not reweighting: they retired those three (relegated to standalone evals) and swapped in three harder/newer ones - AA-Omniscience (knowledge+hallucination, where all but 3 models hallucinate more than they answer correctly), GDPval-AA (real paid-work tasks across 44 professions), and CritPt (physics research problems) - alongside retaining/escalating hard evals (HLE, GPQA Diamond, Terminal-Bench Hard, tau2, SciCode, IFBench, AA-LCR). This re-opened headroom: launch top score dropped to ~50. Notably, by Jun 2026 the frontier has re-climbed to ~60-65 on the live site, showing even a hardened index re-saturates within ~6 months - implying a continuous-refresh cadence is mandatory. Limitation for us: even at launch the best OPEN models sat only ~6 pts behind frontier, so the index de-saturates at the frontier but still compresses near the top and is not designed to spread small local models.
reasoning_vs_nonreasoning_handling: Concrete and directly copyable. Temperature: non-reasoning models = 0; reasoning models = 0.6 (unless the model creator recommends otherwise). Max output tokens: non-reasoning = 16,384 (reduced for models with smaller context windows); reasoning models = the maximum output-token / thinking budget disclosed by the model creator, set as a custom value per reasoning model (i.e. they honor each model's recommended thinking budget rather than imposing one cap). Repeats per question are scaled to variance (1 to 5; reasoning-heavy/high-variance evals get more repeats). For cost/latency accounting on reasoning models, token usage reflects BOTH reasoning and answer-parsing steps (explicitly noted for CritPt), and latency is split into Time-to-First-Token (first reasoning token) vs Time-to-First-Answer-Token (after thinking completes). Models are listed with their effort setting baked into the name (e.g. 'GPT-5.5 (xhigh)', 'Claude Opus 4.8 (Adaptive Reasoning, Max Effort)'), so different reasoning efforts of the same base model appear as distinct leaderboard entries.
normalization_method: Each evaluation is normalized to a comparable 0-1 / 0-100 contribution before the weighted average. Most evals normalize trivially as pass@1 accuracy expressed as a fraction of max (tau2, Terminal-Bench Hard, SciCode, IFBench, HLE, GPQA Diamond, CritPt, AA-LCR all use accuracy/pass@1 aggregated across their repeats, validated by an LLM 'equality checker' where answers are free-form e.g. HLE/AA-LCR). Two evals use bespoke transforms: (1) GDPval-AA produces an ELO from pairwise LLM-judge comparisons, then normalizes via clamp((Elo - 500) / 2000) - so Elo 1400 -> 0.45 - with each model's ELO FROZEN at the time it is added and the scale anchored to GPT-5.1 (Non-Reasoning) = Elo 1000. (2) AA-Omniscience contributes 50% Accuracy + 50% (1 - Hallucination Rate), blending correctness with calibrated abstention (its native Omniscience Index scores +1 correct / -1 wrong-when-answered / 0 abstain). The composite is the sum of (normalized eval score x its weight), yielding a ~0-100 Index.
weighting_method: Hierarchical weighting (introduced in v4.0, replacing v3.0's flatter per-eval weighting). Four capability categories each contribute exactly 25%: Agents, Coding, General, Scientific Reasoning. Within each category, individual evals carry explicit sub-weights that sum to 25%: Agents = GDPval-AA 16.7% + tau2-Bench Telecom 8.3%; Coding = Terminal-Bench Hard 16.7% + SciCode 8.3%; General = AA-Omniscience 12.5% + AA-LCR 6.25% + IFBench 6.25%; Scientific Reasoning = HLE 12.5% + GPQA Diamond 6.25% + CritPt 6.25%. So the headline weights are 16.7% (the two flagship agent/coding evals), 12.5% (AA-Omniscience, HLE), and 6.25-8.3% (the rest). The category-equal-weight design deliberately stops any single easy axis from dominating the composite - the exact failure mode our v0 suite exhibits.
presentation_patterns: Hero chart: Intelligence (y) vs Price/USD-per-1M-tokens (x, log scale) scatter - the signature 'quality-vs-price' view; top-left = 'most attractive quadrant' (high intelligence, low cost). Open-weight vs proprietary points are visually distinguished/filterable.
Intelligence vs Output Speed (tokens/sec) scatter - the 'quality-vs-speed' companion chart.
Intelligence vs Output Tokens Used scatter - a token-efficiency / verbosity view (how many tokens a model burns to reach its score).
Frontier Intelligence Over Time line chart - tracks the rising frontier and shows saturation/headroom across releases.
Filterable leaderboard table - select a subset (e.g. 29 of 529 models) by provider, with an open-weights-vs-proprietary toggle and sortable columns (index, price, speed, context).
Per-model pages and per-provider pages (e.g. /models/<x>, /providers/<x>) with drill-down on each eval.

Intelligence Evaluation Breakdown table - per-eval scores feeding the composite, shown alongside token-usage (input/reasoning/answer) and the literal USD cost to run the full Index suite for that model.
Models named with reasoning-effort/variant baked into the label so each effort setting is a separate row (e.g. 'GPT-5.5 (xhigh)').
critiques_of_field: A hardened index re-saturates fast: v4.0 launched with a 50-point ceiling (down from 73) but within ~6 months the live frontier has climbed to ~60-65 - benchmark de-saturation is temporary and demands a continuous-refresh treadmill, not a one-time rebuild.

The index compresses at the top: best open-weight models sit only ~3-6 points behind frontier (Kimi K2.6/MiMo V2.5 Pro ~54 vs GPT-5.5 xhigh ~60). Good for headlines, weak for fine-grained ranking - and it is built for frontier-scale models, so it does not validate that a composite separates SMALL local models (our actual need).

Heavy reliance on LLM-as-judge: GDPval-AA (pairwise ELO judging), HLE and AA-LCR (LLM equality checkers) all use a model to grade models, importing judge bias/instability into the 'objective' composite.

Agentic evals require infra most users can't reproduce (E2B sandboxes, Terminus 2, dual-control user simulators), so the headline scores are not independently reproducible by a local user on an OpenAI-compatible endpoint - they are effectively a hosted-only benchmark for 4 of 10 evals.

Contamination disclosure is uneven: AA-Omniscience documents a 600-public/5,400-private split, but the methodology page makes no explicit contamination/canary statements for AA-LCR or GDPval-AA, and several adopted evals (GPQA Diamond, HLE) have known web-leakage / adversarial-selection caveats.

Mixing heterogeneous metric types (accuracy fractions, frozen ELO clamps, hallucination-penalized indices) into one 0-100 number is convenient but semantically lossy - a 1-point Index move can mean very different things depending on which eval drove it.

Frozen-at-add-time ELO (GDPval-AA) means a model's agent score never updates as the judge pool or field evolves, so cross-cohort comparisons on that 16.7%-weighted component can drift out of calibration.

source: @scaling01 ("Lisan al Gaib") benchmarking philosophy — X account (auth-walled to WebFetch; reconstructed via WebSearch result snippets, Thread Reader / GitHub mirrors, and secondary press)
summary:
@scaling01 (Lisan al Gaib, https://x.com/scaling01) is a prolific independent LLM-benchmark commentator who has built his own benchmarks (LisanBench, ShizoBench) and curated meta-leaderboards. His central thesis: most "traditional academic" benchmarks (he names MMLU and AIME explicitly) are "saturated and skewed toward niche domains," correlate poorly with general intelligence or practical utility, and get gamed because "labs simply RL-max them," producing inferior shipped models. He therefore (a) trusts a basket of independent / community-run benchmarks over lab-reported numbers, and (b) builds benchmarks designed for "emergent complexity from simple problem scaling" — cheap, programmatically verifiable, contamination-resistant, with "no skill ceiling."

VERIFIED FLAGSHIP — LisanBench (his "new go-to benchmark"): an open-ended Word-Ladder variant. A model is given a start word and must produce the longest chain where each next word is a valid dictionary word at Levenshtein distance exactly 1, no repeats. It deliberately stresses five axes at once — forward planning (avoiding dead-ends, ~longest-simple-path/NP-hard so it scales indefinitely), vocabulary/knowledge breadth, long-range memory (no repeats over hundreds of steps), strict constraint adherence, and "stamina" (models that break early on long generations are exposed). He argues this is superior to MMLU/AIME and an improvement on AidanBench (no embedding model, far cheaper, "trivially verifiable") and SOLO-Bench (explicitly tests knowledge + stronger constraints). Scorer is fully programmatic (dictionary + edit-distance check); default 50 start words x 3 trials (150 calls); a 15-word x 2-trial "reduced" preset correlates R^2=0.997 with full and is ~4-5x cheaper; full sweep ~$50 for ~57 models. Contamination resistance comes from real-time generation under hard math constraints + combinatorial path explosion (memorization infeasible); dictionary is a pinned SCOWL/ESDB list (25 Feb 2026) with a published SHA-256 for reproducibility. He flags a known confound: it operates at character level, so tokenization affects scores ("models with better tokenizers should perform better, all else equal"). LICENSE = custom/non-standard: "attribution required (credit @scaling01 on X)" — NOT a clean OSI license; redistribution by us would need his permission. Repo: github.com/voice-from-the-outer-world/lisan-bench.

VERIFIED CURATION — "The Ultimate LLM Benchmark list" (May 2025 thread): the benches he endorses as signal include SimpleBench, SOLO-Bench, AidanBench, SEAL by Scale (esp. the MultiChallenge leaderboard), LMArena (with Style Control), LiveBench, ARC-AGI, LechMazur's suite (Thematic Generalization, elimination/confabulation games, Misguided Attention), EQBench (esp. Longform Writing), Fiction-Live Bench, MC-Bench, TrackingAI IQ, Dubesor LLM, Balrog-AI, Snake-Bench, Context-Arena, OpenCompass, HHEM; coding/math/agentic: Aider-Polyglot, BigCodeBench, WebDev-Arena, WeirdML, Symflower, PHYBench, MathArena. In that thread he explicitly dismisses the classic academic set as "saturated / no signal" because "labs simply RL-max them." He also built an "Ultimate LLM Meta-Leaderboard averaged across the 28 best benchmarks" (ranked Gemini 2.5 Pro > o3 > Sonnet 3.7 Thinking at the time) — so he is willing to AVERAGE many benches, but his averaging is over hand-picked high-signal independent benches, not lab numbers.

VERIFIED SECOND BENCH — ShizoBench: his own "ask LLMs nonsensical questions and see whether they catch it" benchmark (parallel to Peter Gostev's "Bullshit Benchmark"); rewards models that push back on broken premises rather than confidently elaborating — i.e. a hallucination/uncertainty-discipline discriminator where Anthropic models dominate.

INDEPENDENT-OVER-LAB stance is visible in his running commentary (e.g. highlighting "GPT-5 ranking 7th on Dubesor LLM Benchmark," and that Claude tops Dubesor "due to their strong reasoning category sub-score") and in LisanBench result threads that rank models on their own harness regardless of vendor claims (e.g. Dec 2025: "GPT-5.2 Thinking improves over GPT-5 and o3 but does not match Opus 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2 Speciale or Grok 4").
key_takeaways: CORE THESIS (attributed, LisanBench README): traditional academic benchmarks like MMLU and AIME are 'saturated and skewed toward niche domains' and correlate poorly with 'general intelligence or practical model utility.' In his 'Ultimate Benchmark list' thread he dismisses the classic set as 'saturated / no signal' because 'labs simply RL-max them' -> the model shipped to consumers is worse than the leaderboard implies.

DESIGN PRINCIPLE he advocates: 'emergent complexity from simple problem scaling' + 'no skill ceiling.' Build a simple task whose difficulty scales indefinitely (LisanBench's longest-word-chain is ~NP-hard), so the same eval discriminates from small models up to frontier and does not saturate as models improve. He prizes 'trivial verification' (programmatic scorer, no embedding model, no LLM judge, no ambiguity) and low cost (~$50 for ~57 models; a reduced preset is 4-5x cheaper and still R^2=0.997 with full).

CONTAMINATION stance (LisanBench README): real-time generation under strict mathematical constraints + combinatorial path explosion makes memorization 'infeasible,' so pretraining contamination 'poses minimal risk' — he treats *generative, constraint-checked, combinatorial* tasks as inherently contamination-resistant, and pins/hashes the dictionary (SCOWL/ESDB, dated, SHA-256) for reproducibility. He ALSO endorses private/held-out sets via the benches he trusts (SimpleBench keeps a 200+ private set; SEAL is private).

REASONING vs NON-REASONING: he reports reasoning models gain a 'distinct advantage' on LisanBench via explicit backtracking, and he tracks token-EFFICIENCY as a first-class signal, not just accuracy — e.g. Opus 4 beating o3 on some start words 'with only one third of the reasoning tokens,' and (Dec 2025) noting OpenAI 'lags far behind' Opus 4.5 / Gemini 3 Pro / Grok 4 on reasoning efficiency. Implication for us: report cost/tokens-to-score alongside score.

HALLUCINATION / CALIBRATION as a discriminator (ShizoBench): he built a 'nonsensical-question' bench rewarding models that refuse / flag a broken premise vs confidently confabulating — a robustness axis that strongly separates model families (Anthropic dominates). This is orthogonal to knowledge/math accuracy and resists saturation differently.

INDEPENDENT > LAB-REPORTED: his trusted-bench list is almost entirely independent/community evals (SimpleBench, AidanBench, SOLO-Bench, LiveBench, Dubesor, LechMazur suite, EQBench, ARC-AGI, SEAL). He surfaces independent rankings that contradict lab framing (GPT-5 '7th on Dubesor'). He DOES publish a meta-leaderboard 'averaged across the 28 best benchmarks,' but the inputs are hand-curated high-signal independent benches — averaging is acceptable to him only over vetted signal, not over saturated/gamed components.

He singles out specific SUB-leaderboards rather than whole suites — 'particularly the MultiChallenge leaderboard' (SEAL), 'the Longform writing leaderboard' (EQBench), and attributes Dubesor rankings to a 'reasoning category sub-score.' Lesson: he believes the discriminating signal lives in specific sub-tracks, not in a blended top-line number.
lessons_for_us: DISCRIMINATION via 'no skill ceiling' generative tasks is the most directly applicable idea: our v0 saturates because MCQ axes have a hard ceiling. A LisanBench-style task (programmatic scorer, difficulty that scales indefinitely, single task stressing planning+knowledge+memory+constraint+stamina) is exactly the kind of axis that stays un-saturated from 9B to frontier. It is also cheap and contamination-resistant by construction. STRONGLY consider adopting/adapting a word-ladder or similar combinatorial-generative axis. Caveat: tokenization confound means it may unfairly penalize models with weaker tokenizers — disclose this.

LICENSING WARNING: LisanBench is NOT cleanly redistributable — its terms are custom ('attribution required, credit @scaling01') rather than an OSI license, so we cannot just vendor it. If we want the *mechanism*, we can re-implement the word-ladder idea on our OWN public-domain/clean dictionary (e.g. an explicitly-licensed SCOWL build) and cite him for the concept — avoiding his bespoke terms entirely. Verify any dictionary's license before shipping.

ADOPT his 'averaging is fine only over vetted high-signal components' discipline. Our composite failed partly because it averaged saturated axes (Gemini 'won' by acing easy ones). Mirror his approach: curate per-axis high-signal benches, show the sub-scores prominently, and treat the top-line average as secondary — or drop saturated axes from the composite entirely (matches the 'Signal and Noise' SNR-subsetting literature we also found).

REPORT TOKENS/COST-TO-SCORE, not just accuracy. He treats reasoning-token efficiency as first-class and shows two models with equal scores can differ by an order of magnitude in tokens. For a LOCAL-model leaderboard this is doubly important (local users are compute-bound): a 'score per 1k tokens' or 'score at fixed token budget' column would be a differentiator and would also normalize reasoning vs non-reasoning fairly.

ADD a hallucination/uncertainty-discipline axis (ShizoBench-style nonsense-premise questions). It is programmatic-ish to score (did the model flag the broken premise?), resists saturation, and discriminates by model family in a way knowledge/math axes do not. We'd need to generate our own item set (clean license) and a robust pushback-detection scorer; flag answer-extraction fragility.

PREFER independent/community evals and date-windowed or generated variants over static academic sets — consistent with his RL-max critique and our contamination constraint. Where we must use an academic set, follow the benches he trusts (SimpleBench-style private hold-out) and keep a private sentinel split (we already do this for generated-math; extend the pattern).

His trusted-list is a ready-made shortlist to triage against OUR hard constraints: LiveBench (date-windowed, contamination-resistant, programmatic — strong fit), AidanBench/SOLO-Bench (generative, cheap — but check license/scorer), ARC-AGI (discriminates but agentic-ish and partly private), EQBench-Longform & SEAL-MultiChallenge (need LLM-judge — violates our 'stable programmatic scorer' constraint, treat with caution). Several (SEAL, SimpleBench private set) are NOT redistributable — same trap as GPQA.

Single-task, multi-axis benches reduce harness complexity for LOCAL users (one OpenAI-compatible endpoint, no sandbox/browser/code-exec). This aligns with our LOCAL-RUNNABLE constraint and argues for favouring generative-constraint benches over agentic/coding benches that need sandboxes.

source: aggregators (Epoch / LiveBench / OpenLLM v2 / LMArena / Boggs)
summary: Surveyed how five serious benchmark aggregators build composites, with focus on the two design problems killing our v0 suite: saturation and non-discrimination. The field has converged on two competing answers. (A) STITCHED-DIFFICULTY MODELS (Epoch ECI, Boggs.tech). Epoch's Capabilities Index is the state of the art: it fits a 2-parameter Item Response Theory (IRT) model across 39-41 benchmarks, 147 models, ~1123 evaluations. Each benchmark b gets a DIFFICULTY (Db) and a SLOPE/DISCRIMINATION (alpha_b); each model gets an ability score Cm; predicted performance = sigma(alpha_b*(Cm - Db)). This is the single most important idea for us: a SATURATED easy benchmark is NOT discarded, it still contributes information at the low end via its difficulty/slope, while hard benchmarks (FrontierMath) carry the frontier. The composite never saturates because there is "no maximum achievable ECI" and you keep adding harder benchmarks. The scale is arbitrary/relative (Elo-like), anchored by fixing reference models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet=130, GPT-5=150). Benchmarks must be 0-1 scored and rescaled so random-guessing=0. They aggregate across eval settings by taking the MAX (best reasoning_effort/provider) per benchmark. Boggs.tech mimics ECI on ~40 scraped leaderboards, anchors GPT-4.1=100/GPT-5=150, requires a model appear on >=8 benchmarks, and for Elo-type sources normalizes so top=100% and others = 2*P(win vs top). (B) FRESH/HELD-OUT SUITES (LiveBench). 18 tasks across 6 categories (math, coding, reasoning, language, data analysis, instruction-following); fights contamination by releasing NEW questions monthly drawn from recent arXiv/news/IMDb/datasets, scores against OBJECTIVE ground truth with NO LLM judge, and deliberately keeps difficulty high (top models stay below ~85%, ~55pt spread) so it does not saturate. (C) NORMALIZED-MEAN (HF Open LLM Leaderboard v2, now ARCHIVED Mar 2025). 6 benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GPQA, MuSR, MATH-Lvl5, IFEval, BBH); normalize each via (value-lower)/(upper-lower)*100 where lower = random baseline (1/num_choices, e.g. 0.10 for MMLU-Pro's 10 choices, 0.25 for GPQA), CLIP below-baseline to 0, average normalized subtasks first for multi-subtask benches (MuSR/BBH), then simple-mean the 6. Retired because reasoning models + assistants changed what matters and it was "hill-climbing irrelevant directions" - a direct warning to us. (D) HUMAN-PREFERENCE ELO (LMArena). Bradley-Terry logistic fit over pairwise votes, presented on Elo scale (coef*400+1000); STYLE CONTROL adds response length + markdown headers/lists/bold as regression covariates to de-confound style from substance (length is the dominant confound). Measures likeability not correctness; gameable, self-selected prompts - useful as a cross-check axis, not a capability ground truth.
key_takeaways: Saturation is THE central problem the whole field is fighting: Epoch states benchmarks 'tend to saturate within 1-3 years', which is exactly our v0 failure. Two proven cures exist: (a) stitch many benchmarks of DIFFERENT difficulty into one latent scale (Epoch ECI/IRT), or (b) keep regenerating fresh hard items (LiveBench). A simple normalized mean of fixed benchmarks (Open LLM v2) does NOT cure it and was retired.

Epoch ECI is the gold standard for a composite that never saturates: a 2PL IRT model performance=sigma(alpha_b*(Cm-Db)) gives every benchmark a difficulty Db AND a discrimination/slope alpha_b. Crucially, a benchmark saturated at the frontier still carries discrimination signal at the LOW end - so we should KEEP easy benchmarks for separating small local models, not delete them. 39-41 benchmarks, 147 models, ~1123 evals; ridge penalties lambda_a=0.378, lambda_b=0.0001.

The ECI/Boggs scale is RELATIVE and ANCHOR-PINNED, not absolute: arbitrary units fixed by reference models (Epoch: Claude 3.5 Sonnet=130, GPT-5=150; Boggs: GPT-4.1=100, GPT-5=150). This is exactly our 'anchor models' design - we already run frontier anchors through the identical suite, so we can pin our composite to them and report local models on that frontier-anchored scale. There is explicitly 'no maximum achievable' score.

Normalize-by-random-baseline is the accepted floor treatment (Open LLM v2 + Epoch both do it): normalized = (raw - 1/num_choices)/(1 - 1/num_choices), clip negatives to 0. For multi-subtask benches, normalize each subtask THEN average (their MuSR worked example: 0.7/0.4/0.6 raw across 2/5/3-choice subtasks -> 40/25/40 -> 35.0 overall). This fixes our 'Gemini wins by acing easy axes' bug only partially - normalization alone is NOT enough, you also need difficulty-aware weighting (IRT slope) or the easy axes still dominate.

LiveBench's anti-contamination recipe is directly portable and matches what we already prototyped (generated-math + private sentinel): monthly-refreshed questions from recent arXiv/news/IMDb, objective ground-truth scoring with NO LLM judge, deliberately hard (top models <~85%, ~55pt spread). Their no-LLM-judge stance validates our programmatic-scorer constraint.

Aggregate across inference settings by taking the MAX per benchmark (Epoch): they take the highest score over thinking-effort/provider to capture true capability. We should decide our policy explicitly - our reasoning-effort adapters already vary this, and a max-over-settings rule prevents penalizing a model for a bad default config.

LMArena's style control is a reusable technique for ANY preference/judge axis: put confounders (length, markdown headers/lists/bold) as covariates in the regression so the score reflects substance not formatting; length is the dominant confound. If we ever add an LLM-judged or preference axis, control for length or it becomes a verbosity contest.

Coverage/eligibility gating matters for a community leaderboard: Boggs requires a model to appear on >=8 benchmarks AND >=1/3 of benchmarks available at its release date before it gets a composite; Epoch drops models with <4 benchmark scores (unreliable CIs). We need an analogous rule so a local run that only completed 2 axes doesn't get a misleadingly precise composite.
lessons_for_us: ADOPT a difficulty-stitched composite, not a flat normalized mean. Our 'a 9B is within 6pts of SOTA' failure is the textbook symptom of averaging saturated axes. Fit (or approximate) an IRT-style model where each axis carries a difficulty and a discrimination weight, so hard axes drive frontier separation and easy axes drive small-model separation. Even a lightweight 2PL fit over our anchors+community runs would beat a mean.

KEEP a difficulty ladder spanning small-local to frontier on every capability. Per Epoch's 'stitching' insight, we want easy AND hard benchmarks in each domain (e.g. math: GSM8K-class -> competition -> FrontierMath-class). Saturated-at-frontier is fine and useful as long as something harder sits above it; the bug is when the HARDEST axis is still saturated for small models too.

PIN the composite to our frontier anchors with an arbitrary fixed scale (e.g. set two anchors to fixed values like Epoch/Boggs). This gives a stable, interpretable 'distance below frontier' number for local models and survives adding new harder benchmarks later without rescaling history.

DON'T rely on normalization alone to fix the easy-axis-dominance bug. Open LLM v2 normalized by random baseline and STILL got retired for being saturated/irrelevant. Normalization fixes the floor; difficulty-weighting (IRT slope) fixes the ceiling. We need both.

BUILD contamination resistance as a first-class, refreshing component (LiveBench model): keep our generated-math + private sentinel, add date-windowed fresh items, and never use an LLM judge - objective ground-truth scoring only, which we already do. This is a genuine product differentiator for a community leaderboard.

DEFINE an explicit inference-setting policy (Epoch's max-over-settings): for anchors and local runs, decide whether the composite uses default config or best-of (reasoning_effort, quant, sampler). Max-over-settings measures capability; default measures out-of-box UX. Pick one and label it; our reasoning-effort adapters make either feasible.

ADD eligibility gating + confidence intervals to the leaderboard (Boggs/Epoch): require a minimum number of completed axes before showing a composite, drop/flag low-coverage runs, and show CIs (bootstrap) so noisy small-sample local runs aren't presented as precise. Our v1 scoring already has paired-delta bootstrap - surface it.

TREAT human-preference Elo (LMArena) as an optional cross-check axis with style control, NOT as ground truth. It rewards style/likeability and is gameable; if we add any preference/judge signal, regress out length and formatting first, and keep it separate from the objective-scored composite.
benchmarks_used: Epoch ECI underlying set (39-41 benchmarks): FrontierMath Tiers 1-3 & Tier 4 (v2), SWE-bench Verified, GPQA Diamond, OTIS Mock AIME 2024-2025, MATH Level 5, SimpleQA Verified, Chess Puzzles, GSM8K (and ~30 more)

LiveBench (18 tasks / 6 categories): math (AMC/AIME-style + synthesis), coding (code_completion + code_generation/LCB), reasoning (web_of_lies_v2, zebra/spatial puzzles), language (typos, connections, plot-unscrambling), data analysis (table reformat/predict/join), instruction following
HF Open LLM Leaderboard v2 (6): MMLU-Pro, GPQA, MuSR, MATH Lvl 5, IFEval, BBH
LMArena/Chatbot Arena: pairwise human-preference votes (text/vision/etc arenas)
Boggs.tech: ~40 scraped public leaderboards aggregated ECI-style (METR Time Horizon explicitly excluded)
normalization_method: Three distinct families observed. (1) IRT/latent-ability (Epoch ECI, Boggs.tech): benchmarks rescaled to 0-1 with random-guess rescaled to 0; fit 2PL model performance(m,b)=sigma(alpha_b*(Cm-Db)) estimating per-model ability Cm and per-benchmark difficulty Db + discrimination/slope alpha_b by minimizing binary cross-entropy with ridge penalties (lambda_a=0.378, lambda_b=0.0001); scale is arbitrary/relative, pinned by anchoring reference models. (2) Normalize-by-random-baseline then mean (Open LLM v2): normalize_within_range(value,lower,higher)=(value-lower)/(higher-lower), *100; lower=1/num_choices (MMLU-Pro 0.10, GPQA 0.25), upper=1.0; clip to 0 if raw<lower; for multi-subtask benches normalize each subtask then average; final = simple mean of the 6 normalized benchmark scores. (3) Elo->% conversion (Boggs for preference sources): top model=100%, others=2*P(model beats top model). LMArena: Bradley-Terry coefficients mapped to Elo via coef*400+1000 (cosmetic).
weighting_method: Two paradigms. Epoch ECI and Boggs.tech use IMPLICIT difficulty-aware weighting: the IRT slope (alpha_b) and difficulty (Db) determine how much each benchmark informs a given ability range, so benchmarks auto-weight by where they discriminate (hard benches dominate at the frontier, easy benches at the low end) - no hand-set weights. Epoch additionally aggregates across eval settings by taking the MAX (best thinking-effort/provider) per benchmark before fitting. Open LLM Leaderboard v2 uses EQUAL weighting: unweighted arithmetic mean of the 6 normalized scores (and equal-weight mean of subtasks within MuSR/BBH). LiveBench uses equal-weight averaging within and across its 6 categories. LMArena has no benchmark weighting (single preference signal); style features enter as additive regression covariates.
handles_saturation_how: Epoch ECI (best answer): explicitly built because 'benchmarks tend to saturate within 1-3 years'; stitches benchmarks of widely varying difficulty (GSM8K -> OTIS Mock AIME -> FrontierMath) onto one latent scale so a benchmark saturated at the frontier still supplies discrimination lower down, and there is 'no maximum achievable ECI' - you keep adding harder benchmarks. Boggs.tech inherits this and prefers 'unbounded' tasks (Elo/profit-based). LiveBench fights saturation by construction: deliberately hard tasks (top models <~85%, ~55pt spread) refreshed monthly so the ceiling keeps moving. Open LLM Leaderboard v2 did NOT solve saturation (its blog title: 'performances are plateauing, let's make the leaderboard steep again' - it picked harder benchmarks but a fixed normalized-mean still saturated and was archived Mar 2025). LMArena: preference Elo doesn't saturate the same way but conflates style with capability. LESSON: difficulty-stitching (IRT) and/or continuous fresh-item generation beat flat normalized means.
reasoning_vs_nonreasoning_handling: Epoch ECI: aggregates across evaluation settings including 'thinking effort', taking the highest score per benchmark, so a reasoning model is scored at its best-effort capability rather than a fixed budget; the IRT scale places reasoning and non-reasoning models on the same ability axis. This is the explicit cause cited for retiring Open LLM Leaderboard v2 ('hello reasoning and LM assistants') - its fixed few-shot log-likelihood/generative format did not suit reasoning models and made the leaderboard obsolete (archived Mar 2025), a direct warning that a frozen non-reasoning-shaped suite ages badly. LiveBench scores reasoning and non-reasoning identically via objective ground-truth (no special-casing). LMArena compares whatever the model outputs, so reasoning models' longer/structured answers are partly a STYLE effect that style-control regresses out. For us: our reasoning-effort adapters + max-over-settings (Epoch-style) policy is the right pattern; pick and label whether the composite is best-of-effort (capability) or default (UX).
confidence_interval_treatment: Epoch ECI: reports CIs and explicitly DROPS models with <4 benchmark scores to avoid 'unreliable CIs' / low-certainty estimates; 90% confidence intervals shown as shaded bands when mapping ECI to expected per-benchmark performance. LMArena/Chatbot Arena: bootstrap-style uncertainty intervals that WIDEN for models with fewer pairwise comparisons; 2025 research notes rankings can flip from dropping a handful of preference votes (fragility of the tail). Boggs.tech: coverage gate (>=8 benchmarks AND >=1/3 of benchmarks available at release date) acts as an eligibility/CI-quality filter. Open LLM Leaderboard v2: did not emphasize per-model CIs in the public table (deterministic eval, single run). LiveBench: objective scoring over fixed monthly question sets; sampling variance from finite question counts not prominently surfaced as CIs. LESSON for us: we already have paired-delta bootstrap in v1 scoring - surface CIs on the leaderboard and gate/flag low-coverage local runs.
presentation_patterns: Single headline composite number on a relative, anchor-pinned scale (Epoch ECI ~150 for frontier; Boggs GPT-4.1=100/GPT-5=150) - one sortable column users trust, with the arbitrary-units caveat stated.

Composite ALWAYS decomposable into per-category/per-benchmark scores beneath it (LiveBench's 6 categories; Open LLM v2's 6 benchmarks) so users can see WHY a model ranks where it does - avoids the black-box composite problem.

Difficulty-vs-capability explainer charts: Epoch maps one ECI score to expected performance on several benchmarks of different difficulty (GSM8K/AIME/FrontierMath) with 90% CI bands - teaches users that the same composite means different raw scores per benchmark.

Explicit contamination/freshness framing as a trust signal: LiveBench foregrounds 'contamination-limited, monthly-refreshed, no LLM judge'; we should badge our generated/sentinel/date-windowed axes the same way.
Eligibility + uncertainty shown, not hidden: coverage gates (min benchmarks) and widening CIs for thin data (Epoch/LMArena) so noisy community runs aren't presented as precise.
Style/confound transparency (LMArena): a toggle between raw and style-controlled rankings shows users the score is de-biased - analogous to us showing default vs best-of-settings.
critiques_of_field: Flat normalized-mean composites (Open LLM Leaderboard v2) saturate and get retired even after deliberately picking 'harder' benchmarks - the title literally admits 'performances are plateauing'; equal-weighting hard and easy axes lets easy axes dominate, which is exactly our v0 bug. Normalization-by-random-baseline alone does NOT fix it.

Human-preference Elo (LMArena) measures likeability/style not correctness, has self-selected prompts and undisclosed anti-gaming heuristics, is gameable via selective submissions/variants, and top rankings can flip by dropping a handful of votes - not a capability ground truth, only a UX cross-check.

IRT composites (Epoch ECI) are powerful but RELATIVE and arbitrary-scaled (no absolute meaning, anchored to reference models), and depend on a dense, overlapping evaluation matrix - they need many models x many benchmarks to fit well, which a young community leaderboard won't have on day one. They also inherit any bias/contamination in their component benchmarks.

Aggregating ~40 third-party leaderboards (Boggs.tech) inherits every upstream methodology inconsistency, contamination, and self-reported-number problem; it's a meta-layer of trust on sources of uneven quality (it even excludes METR Time Horizon by hand).

'Contamination-free' is overclaimed - LiveBench renamed itself 'contamination-LIMITED' in review; monthly refresh reduces but cannot eliminate contamination, and fresh-item generation can drift in difficulty month to month.

Most aggregators under-surface sampling uncertainty: deterministic single-run scores (Open LLM v2) and finite monthly question sets (LiveBench) are presented without CIs, overstating precision - a trap we must avoid given noisy local hardware/quant runs.
access_notes: VERIFIED via WebFetch/WebSearch: Epoch ECI methodology (2PL IRT formula sigma(alpha_b*(Cm-Db)), ridge penalties lambda_a=0.378/lambda_b=0.0001, 39-41 benchmarks/147 models/~1123 evals, scale anchors Claude 3.5 Sonnet=130/GPT-5=150, max-over-settings, no max score) from epoch.ai/benchmarks, /benchmarks/eci, /data-insights/interpreting-eci; Open LLM Leaderboard v2 normalization formula + MuSR worked example + clip-to-0 + retirement reasons/date (archived 13 Mar 2025, >13,000 models) from the huggingface/leaderboards normalization.md and the official closure discussion #1135; LiveBench 6 categories/18 tasks + monthly-refresh sources + no-LLM-judge + unsaturated spread from livebench.ai, the LiveBench GitHub README, and llm-stats; Boggs.tech 2*P(win) Elo normalization + GPT-4.1=100/GPT-5=150 anchoring + >=8-benchmark coverage rule + ECI-methodology basis from boggs.tech/posts/benchmarks; LMArena Bradley-Terry + Elo mapping (coef*400+1000) + online-vs-batch from arena.ai/blog/extended-arena, and style-control covariates (length dominant, + markdown headers/lists/bold) from lmsys.org and news.lmarena.ai. COULD NOT FULLY VERIFY: the LiveBench PDF and HF v2 blog rendered as binary/JS and were read only via secondary sources and the GitHub README (exact per-category->overall averaging formula inferred as equal-weight mean-of-category-means, not quoted verbatim); CURRENT (June 2026) LiveBench leaderboard numbers - llm-stats showed a STALE o3-mini-era snapshot (o3-mini 0.846 top, Qwen3-235B 0.771, bottom 0.296), used only to confirm structure + non-saturation, not as current SOTA; the x.com style-control post is auth-walled (routed around via lmsys.org/news.lmarena.ai mirrors); exact current ECI top scores not displayed on the pages fetched (GPT-5.5 Pro=159 per an Epoch brief seen in search, not independently confirmed on the ECI page).

axis_summary:
The discrimination problem this axis must solve is real and getting worse: the project's symptom (a 9B within 6 pts of frontier on a 112-item MMLU-Pro subset) is the predictable result of two compounding facts. (1) MMLU-Pro is now near-saturated at the frontier (top models cluster ~88-94%; Qwen3.7 Max 89.6%, Claude Opus 4.5 89.5%, Gemini 3 Pro ~90.1%), AND (2) modern SMALL open models are themselves strong on it (Qwen3.5-9B ~82.5% on MMLU-Pro). When the floor is high and the ceiling is compressed, the usable dynamic range collapses to single digits and a tiny fixed subset adds ±7-14pt CIs on top. No single MCQ knowledge bench fixes this anymore.

The way out is a TIERED ladder of difficulty within the axis, scored as a profile rather than one scalar, so each band of model capability lands on a sub-test where it is NOT pinned to floor or ceiling:
  - EASY/MID floor-setter (keeps the bottom of the local range separated, and gives a stable contamination-comparison surface): keep MMLU-Pro (MIT, clean) but as ONE rung, reported with CIs, NOT as the whole axis.
  - HARD discriminator that is LICENSE-CLEAN and runs on any OpenAI-compatible endpoint via generate-then-extract: SuperGPQA (ODC-BY, 26,529 Qs, 13 disciplines). This is the standout finding — it is the only large, open-licensed, genuinely hard knowledge bench that still discriminates cleanly ACROSS the local-to-frontier range (Qwen2.5-14B ~38% overall / 20% on its hard split, DeepSeek-R1 ~62%, frontier ~72%), has a built-in difficulty stratification (easy/middle/hard) you can exploit directly, and ships first-class non-English-exam content so it is not just an MMLU-Pro reskin. It should be the new HARD knowledge rung and arguably the single most valuable addition to the suite.
  - REASONING discriminator (pure deduction, low knowledge-contamination surface): BBEH (BIG-Bench Extra Hard, Apache-2.0 code + CC-BY data) is the correct successor to the now-saturated BBH (BBH 90%+, retired). BBEH spreads frontier from ~10% (general) to ~45% (reasoning-specialist) — excellent frontier discrimination — BUT small local models sit near chance, so it is a CEILING-band rung, not a bottom-of-range one. Adopt as a "maybe/stretch" rung with normalization, knowing it floors sub-30B models.

Benchmarks to EXCLUDE and why, bluntly:
  - GPQA (any subset): CC-BY-4.0 metadata but HF-GATED with an explicit click-through agreement "NOT to reveal examples ... online." Serving questions through your public API violates the authors' stated intent regardless of the nominal license. The project already excluded it; that call is correct and now fully sourced. Diamond is also only 198 Qs and near-saturated (frontier high-80s to ~94%).
  - HLE: MIT and has a text-only subset, but (a) small local models floor at <5% (Nova Pro 4.40%) so it gives you nothing across the local range, (b) it REQUIRES an LLM judge (run_judge_results.py) which breaks the "no extra API / fully local" constraint and adds a non-deterministic scorer, and (c) an independent audit found ~30% of text chem/bio answers may be wrong (hence HLE-Rolling). Keep as cited published context only.
  - DROP: CC-BY-SA-4.0 (share-alike copyleft — a redistribution nuance to flag if you serve it) and saturated (a known "solved" reading-comprehension bench); its bag-of-words F1 scorer is also extraction-fragile. Exclude from the ranked suite.
  - ARC-AGI-1/2: genuinely discriminating (ARC-AGI-2 still separates frontier: GPT-5.5 ~85%, Gemini 3.1 Pro ~77%) and public sets are open-licensed (ARC-AGI-1 Apache-2.0; winning solutions MIT/CC0). BUT it is grid-puzzle JSON, not a chat-QA bench: out-of-distribution for a knowledge/reasoning MCQ suite, needs grid-output parsing, and PURE single-call LLMs (no program search) score far below the headline numbers — the famous scores come from agentic/program-search harnesses you can't run unattended on a user's box. Treat as a deferred specialist module, not core axis.
  - MuSR: CC-BY-4.0 and local-runnable, but only 756 items and now saturating at the top (Kimi K2 ~76%); the small fixed size means wide CIs. At most a minor "maybe" rung; SuperGPQA dominates it on every axis except contamination-novelty.
  - SimpleQA (factuality): MIT, 4,326 Qs — a DIFFERENT axis (parametric factual recall / hallucination), worth a dedicated factuality sub-axis, but it requires a GPT-4.1-class LLM grader (breaks fully-local scoring) and frontier is still <60% (Gemini 2.5 Pro F1 55.6). Consider SimpleQA-Verified (1,000 Qs, Google) if you add a factuality axis; needs the same judge caveat.

Bottom line: replace the single saturated MMLU-Pro axis with a 2-3 rung knowledge/reasoning ladder — MMLU-Pro (floor, keep) + SuperGPQA hard split (the new discriminating core) + optionally BBEH-mini (frontier-only stretch) — all generate-then-extract, all license-clean (MIT / ODC-BY / Apache+CC-BY), reported as a stratified profile with bootstrap CIs and difficulty subgroups. This recovers dynamic range at BOTH ends without breaking the local-runnable or licensing constraints, and the project's existing private-genmath sentinel pattern should be mirrored with a small private hold-out per rung as the contamination canary. Caveat to verify in-house: a few frontier GPQA/MMLU-Pro figures on aggregator sites (benchlm.ai, intuitionlabs) carry fabricated-looking model names ("Autopoiesis Aristotle-X1", "The AI Forger") and unstated provenance — trust Epoch AI / official cards / your own anchor runs over those.

benchmarks.name: MMLU-Pro
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multitask knowledge + reasoning across 14 disciplines; 10-option MCQ (vs MMLU's 4), reasoning-augmented (CoT helps ~+19pts), 12,032 questions.
benchmarks.license: MIT (TIGER-Lab HF dataset card, verified 2026-06-13)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Fully ungated MIT data; runs against any OpenAI-compatible endpoint via generate-then-extract (CoT prompt, parse final letter). This is the project's current path and already works on the 5090. No logprobs/loglikelihood required (that lm-eval-harness multichoice path is NOT portable to closed anchor APIs, but the generate path is).
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated/near-saturated: frontier clusters ~88-94% (Qwen3.7 Max 89.6, Claude Opus 4.5 89.5, Gemini 3 Pro ~90.1). Band too narrow to rank top models. Source: benchlm.ai, kili-technology 2026, Ouroboros survey.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: NO floor effect at all — modern small open models score high (Qwen3.5-9B ~82.5). High floor + compressed ceiling = the project's exact dynamic-range collapse. This is the core reason it must become a RUNG, not the whole axis.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med-high: static QA, MIT/widely-mirrored, plateaus ~2yr post-release per saturation studies; some MCQ shortcut-exploitability (Chandak et al. 2025 advocate free-form matching). Mitigate with private hold-out sentinel + difficulty stratification.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
used_by: HF Open LLM Leaderboard v2 (5-shot multichoice)
benchmarks.notes: Keep as the EASY/MID floor-setting rung and contamination-comparison surface, reported with CIs and as a sub-score — NOT as the standalone axis. Pair with a hard rung (SuperGPQA) to restore range. Consider MMLU-Redux (5,700 re-annotated, fixes label errors) or free-form answer matching to reduce MCQ shortcut gaming.

benchmarks.name: SuperGPQA
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Graduate-level knowledge + reasoning across 285 subfields / 72 fields / 13 disciplines; 26,529 MCQ questions with a built-in easy/middle/hard difficulty stratification. Human-LLM collaborative filtering, 80+ expert annotators.
benchmarks.license: ODC-BY (Open Data Commons Attribution; authors assert no copyright over underlying content). Code on GitHub (SuperGPQA/SuperGPQA). Verified 2026-06-13.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Open ungated dataset, plain MCQ — runs on any OpenAI-compatible endpoint via generate-then-extract exactly like MMLU-Pro. Supported in EvalScope; no sandbox, no judge, no code-exec. Large enough (26.5k) to draw a fixed stratified subset with tight CIs.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial/discriminating: frontier ~0.72 (Qwen3.6 Plus 0.716), DeepSeek-R1 61.82 overall — still meaningful headroom and spread at the top, better than MMLU-Pro. Hard split lower (DeepSeek-R1 ~56.9 hard). Source: llm-stats, arXiv 2502.14739.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Discriminates the local range well: Qwen2.5-14B ~44.8 easy / 37.9 mid / 20.0 hard. Small models are clearly separated from mid and frontier across difficulty bands — no floor collapse, no ceiling collapse. This is its key advantage.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low-med: released Feb 2025, very large + broad (285 subfields incl. light industry/agriculture/service domains rarely in training corpora), harder to fully memorize than MMLU. Still static MCQ — mirror the genmath private-sentinel pattern with a held-out slice.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
used_by: increasingly cited on 2025-26 model cards as the hard-knowledge complement to MMLU-Pro/GPQA
benchmarks.notes: STANDOUT FINDING — the single best addition to this axis. Open license (ODC-BY) where GPQA fails, large where Diamond is only 198 Qs, and its native easy/middle/hard split lets you draw a fixed stratified item set that separates 7B-to-frontier on one bench. Make the HARD split the discriminating core rung; report overall + per-difficulty profile.

benchmarks.name: GPQA (Diamond / Main / Extended)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Google-proof graduate-level science (bio/physics/chem) MCQ. Diamond = 198 hardest items (both experts right, non-experts wrong). PhD baseline ~69.7%.
benchmarks.license: CC-BY-4.0 metadata BUT HF dataset is GATED with a mandatory click-through: 'You agree to NOT reveal examples from this dataset in plain text or images online, to reduce the risk of leakage into foundation model training corpora.' Canary string present (gpqa:4b24:...). Verified 2026-06-13.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Two blockers: (1) SERVING blocker — the project would have to redistribute questions through its public API, which directly violates the authors' stated no-republication agreement (the nominal CC-BY does not override the gating intent; community-standing risk). (2) Access blocker — HF gating means users can't pull it without accepting the same agreement. Both fatal for a public community suite.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: near-saturated: frontier clusters high-80s to ~94% (Grok 4 87±2 per Epoch; Gemini 3.1 Pro ~94 per aggregators), well above the 69.7% PhD baseline. Ouroboros/llm-stats project full saturation within ~18-24 months.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Mid/small open models score lower (some sources cite Qwen3.5-9B ~81.7 GPQA-Diamond — verify, likely optimistic), so it would discriminate IF servable — but the licensing kills it regardless.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med, actively mitigated by gating + canary; but 198 items is tiny and high-profile, so leakage pressure is severe. Irrelevant given exclusion.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
used_by: HF Open LLM Leaderboard v2 (0-shot multichoice)
benchmarks.notes: Project's existing exclusion is CORRECT and now fully sourced: gated + explicit no-republication request makes serving it via API a community-norm violation. Keep ONLY as cited published context on model pages (labeled reported, never charted on a measured axis). SuperGPQA is the open-licensed replacement for this slot.

benchmarks.name: Humanity's Last Exam (HLE)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Frontier-difficulty closed-ended expert questions across many subjects; 2,500 public Qs + maintained PRIVATE test set; text-only subset for non-multimodal models.
benchmarks.license: MIT (cais/hle HF). Public set MIT; a private hold-out exists for overfitting checks.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Data is MIT + has a text-only subset (good), and questions are closed-ended. BUT scoring REQUIRES an LLM judge (run_judge_results.py / GPT-class grader) — this breaks the project's 'fully local, no extra API, deterministic scorer' constraint and injects a non-reproducible scorer. Frontier-only difficulty also means it yields zero signal across the local range.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating at the very top and far from saturated (Claude Fable 5 64.5%, Gemini 3.1 Pro 46.44%, GPT-5.4 Pro 44.32%) — but that is irrelevant to a local-model suite.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: SEVERE FLOOR EFFECT — small models score <5% (Nova Pro 4.40%). Local 7B-30B models would sit at ~0-3%, giving no discrimination across the project's actual user range. This is the disqualifier.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low (private hold-out + frontier difficulty), BUT data-quality issue: independent 2025 audit found ~30% of text-only chem/bio answers may be wrong → team launched HLE-Rolling. Noisy gold answers hurt a programmatic scorer.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no (but needs an LLM-judge API call per item, which is the practical equivalent of an external dependency)
used_by: frontier model cards as the headline 'hardest' bench
benchmarks.notes: Exclude from the runnable suite (floor + LLM-judge + answer-noise). Keep as cited published context only. If a frontier-only stretch rung is ever wanted for ANCHORS specifically (not local users), HLE-text could be an anchor-only diagnostic, but the LLM-judge non-determinism still argues against it.

benchmarks.name: BBH (BIG-Bench Hard)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: 23 hard reasoning tasks (algorithmic, multi-step, symbolic, language) drawn from BIG-Bench; CoT-amenable.
benchmarks.license: MIT (BBH data). Apache-2.0 in some mirrors.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Open, MIT, mostly free-form/short-answer + some MCQ — runs via generate-then-extract on any endpoint. lm-eval-harness has it (OLLv2 used 3-shot multichoice). No code-exec.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: SATURATED — frontier 90%+ on most tasks; explicitly retired in favor of BBEH. Not discriminating at the top. Source: BBEH paper (arXiv 2502.19187), LXT 2026.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Mixed: aggregate is moderate for small models (it was a useful OLLv2 rung), but because the frontier ceiling is gone, the usable range is collapsing the same way MMLU-Pro is. Per-task variance is high.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med-high: old (2022), MIT, heavily mirrored, BIG-Bench canary often stripped in copies. Memorization pressure significant.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
used_by: HF Open LLM Leaderboard v2 (one of 6 components)
benchmarks.notes: Superseded. Don't adopt vanilla BBH — adopt BBEH instead for the reasoning rung. Mentioned here because OLLv2 legitimized this whole stack (BBH+IFEval+MATH+GPQA+MuSR+MMLU-Pro); the project is essentially rebuilding a license-clean, anchor-comparable version of OLLv2's design.

benchmarks.name: BBEH (BIG-Bench Extra Hard)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: 23 novel tasks each replacing a BBH task with a much harder variant of the same reasoning skill; pure reasoning, low world-knowledge surface. Full 4,520 items; mini subset 460 (20/task).
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (code) + CC-BY-4.0 (data/materials), google-deepmind/bbeh. Verified 2026-06-13.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Open Apache+CC-BY, programmatic scorer (evaluate.py), free-form answers parsed deterministically — runs via generate-then-extract on any OpenAI-compatible endpoint, no code-exec, no judge. Mini (460) is a ready-made fixed item set. (Not yet on HF as of mid-2026 — pull from GitHub.)
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: STRONGLY discriminating at frontier: best general-purpose model ~9.8% harmonic-avg, best reasoning-specialist ~44.8% — wide spread, far from saturated. Best frontier-ranking signal of any open reasoning bench here.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: FLOOR RISK — paper notes many smaller models perform near chance. Sub-30B local models will mostly floor, so it discriminates the TOP of the range, not the bottom. Use normalization and treat as a ceiling-band rung.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low: released 2025, novel tasks designed to defeat memorization, reasoning-not-recall. Among the most contamination-resistant options on this axis.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.notes: Adopt as the optional HARD REASONING / frontier-stretch rung (use the 460-item mini as the fixed set). It restores frontier discrimination that BBH lost and is contamination-resistant, but it floors small local models — so it informs the top of the leaderboard, not the bottom. Pair with SuperGPQA-hard (mid) and MMLU-Pro (floor) for full-range coverage.

benchmarks.name: MuSR
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multistep soft reasoning over ~1,000-word natural-language narratives (murder mysteries, object placement, team allocation); neurosymbolic synthetic-to-natural generation. 756 MCQ items.
benchmarks.license: CC-BY-4.0 (TAUR-Lab, UT Austin). Verified 2026-06-13.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Open CC-BY, multiple-choice, programmatic scoring — runs via generate-then-extract on any endpoint, no code-exec/judge. Was an OLLv2 component (0-shot multichoice).
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial/saturating: Kimi K2 ~0.764 leads; top models climbing toward ceiling. Long-context narrative reasoning is no longer a frontier separator.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Discriminates mid-range somewhat (it was useful in OLLv2), but only 756 items + 3-way-ish MCQ means chance is high (~33-50% depending on task) and CIs are wide on any fixed subset. Limited bottom-end resolution.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med: generated narratives give some novelty, but released 2023, CC-BY, widely mirrored as an OLLv2 task. The generation method could be re-run for fresh variants (like the project's genmath approach).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
used_by: HF Open LLM Leaderboard v2 (one of 6 components)
benchmarks.notes: A defensible minor rung for narrative/long-context-lite soft reasoning, and its synthetic generator is philosophically aligned with the project's generated-math sentinel idea (could regenerate private variants). But small fixed size + saturating ceiling make it strictly weaker than SuperGPQA for this axis. Adopt only if you want a distinct narrative-reasoning flavor; otherwise skip.

benchmarks.name: DROP
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Discrete reasoning over paragraphs (reading comprehension requiring counting, sorting, arithmetic over a passage); ~9.5k public dev items; numeracy-focused macro-F1 scorer.
benchmarks.license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 (share-alike copyleft — note the redistribution obligation if served). AllenAI, 2019.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Open data, runs on any endpoint via generation. BUT the bag-of-words/number-mismatch F1 scorer is extraction-fragile (HF OLLv1 had a known 'DROP deep dive' where formatting/normalization heavily swung scores), so it needs careful answer-normalization to be reproducible. Share-alike license also adds a redistribution wrinkle vs MMLU-Pro's clean MIT.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated — DROP is on the 'solved' list of reading-comprehension benches (>80% across families); no frontier discrimination. Source: arXiv survey 2508.15361, Ouroboros.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: High floor too (it was an OLLv1-era bench); compressed range like MMLU/HellaSwag. Little discrimination at either end now.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high: 2019 vintage, CC-BY-SA, extremely widely mirrored and trained on. Among the most contaminated options.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
used_by: HF Open LLM Leaderboard v1 (was removed/deprioritized partly due to scorer fragility)
benchmarks.notes: Exclude: saturated, contamination-heavy, fragile F1 extraction, and CC-BY-SA share-alike adds friction. Nothing it offers isn't better covered by SuperGPQA (knowledge) or BBEH (reasoning). Historical interest only.

benchmarks.name: ARC-AGI-1
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Abstraction/reasoning grid puzzles (infer transformation from 3 demo input-output grids, produce exact output grid). 400 public train + 400 public eval tasks; JSON grids 1x1-30x30, values 0-9.
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (fchollet/ARC-AGI public set); ARC Prize winning solutions MIT/CC0. Verified 2026-06-13.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Public set is open + scoring is programmatic (exact grid match), AND it CAN be driven over an OpenAI-compatible chat endpoint (arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking supports OpenAI/custom). BUT it is grid-JSON output, not chat-QA — needs robust grid parsing/extraction (high parser-fragility for non-instruction-tuned local models), is out-of-distribution for a knowledge/reasoning MCQ suite, and the headline scores come from program-search/agentic harnesses, NOT single calls.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated for the headline metric — frontier ~90-95% (GPT-5.5 ~95) via heavy test-time compute/program search; effectively retired as a frontier separator (that's why ARC-AGI-2/3 exist).
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floor for plain local models: pure single-call LLMs score far below the agentic numbers; small models near 0 without search. No usable bottom-end signal in a simple-call setup.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med: public eval set is fixed and old (2019); leakage exists, hence the private competition set. Not relevant given OOD format.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no for scoring (exact match), but competitive scores require program-synthesis/search scaffolding that is itself code-heavy
benchmarks.notes: Out of scope for THIS axis. Genuinely interesting and open-licensed, but it is a grid-puzzle/program-synthesis benchmark, not knowledge/reasoning QA — wrong I/O shape, parser-fragile, and single-call numbers (the only thing a local user could run unattended) are uninformative. Defer to a possible standalone 'abstract reasoning' module if demand appears.

benchmarks.name: ARC-AGI-2
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Harder compositional abstraction grid puzzles (2025). Public + semi-private + private splits; same JSON-grid format, exact-match scoring.
benchmarks.license: Public set open (arcprize/ARC-AGI-2 repo); winning solutions MIT/CC0; eval runs offline on Kaggle without external API for the prize track.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Same as ARC-AGI-1: public set is open + exact-match programmatic, and can be driven via chat API, BUT grid-JSON I/O is OOD for this suite, parser-fragile, and the real scores need program-search harnesses. The prize evaluation is deliberately Kaggle-offline (no external API), which is the opposite of the project's BYO-endpoint model.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: DISCRIMINATING at the frontier (rare): GPT-5.5 ~85%, GPT-5.4 Pro ~83.3%, Gemini 3.1 Pro ~77.1% (June 2026); started at ~24% best-competition in 2025. Genuinely separates top reasoning systems — but via heavy compute, not single calls.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floor for plain local models — base single-call LLMs and small models are near 0; the spread that exists is among frontier+search systems. No bottom-of-range signal.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low for private/semi-private; public split fixed. Strong contamination design overall.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no for exact-match scoring; competitive performance needs program-synthesis scaffolding
benchmarks.notes: Best frontier-discriminating reasoning bench surveyed — but still wrong shape for a local chat-QA suite and uninformative at the local model range in a single-call setting. Keep as cited published context; revisit ONLY as a deferred specialist 'abstract reasoning' module (Docker/agentic, opt-in), aligned with the project's existing 'defer agentic/code-exec modules' stance.

benchmarks.name: SimpleQA / SimpleQA-Verified
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Short-form parametric factuality / hallucination resistance: adversarially-collected fact-seeking questions with single indisputable answers. SimpleQA 4,326 Qs (OpenAI); SimpleQA-Verified 1,000 Qs (Google, de-noised).
benchmarks.license: MIT (basicv8vc/SimpleQA). SimpleQA-Verified: Google release (check card; CC-BY-style).
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Data is open, but official scoring uses a GPT-4.1-class LLM grader to judge correct/incorrect/not-attempted — breaks the 'fully local, deterministic, no extra API' constraint. A strict string/alias match is possible but loses fidelity. Different AXIS from knowledge/reasoning (it's factual recall / calibration).
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating / not saturated: frontier still <60% (Gemini 2.5 Pro F1 55.6; GPT-4o <40). Measures hallucination, which stays hard — good headroom.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Small models score low (limited parametric knowledge) but not strictly zero; gives a real hallucination-rate signal. Reasonable spread, though smaller models cluster low.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med: static factual QA, MIT, mirrored; SimpleQA-Verified reduces label noise. Facts are also time-sensitive (answers can go stale).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no (but LLM-judge call per item in the official pipeline)
benchmarks.notes: Out of the core knowledge/reasoning axis, but a strong candidate for a SEPARATE factuality/hallucination sub-axis if the project adds one (the project's v0 doesn't have factuality). Prefer SimpleQA-Verified (1,000 Qs, cleaner). Caveat: needs an LLM grader for fidelity — would require relaxing the no-extra-API rule or accepting strict-match degradation. Flag as a future axis, not a fix for the current saturation problem.

top_picks_for_local_suite: SuperGPQA (ODC-BY) — adopt the easy/middle/HARD stratified split as the new discriminating knowledge core; only large open-licensed hard-knowledge bench that separates 7B-to-frontier on one bench and runs via generate-then-extract on any OpenAI-compatible endpoint
MMLU-Pro (MIT) — KEEP but demote to the EASY/MID floor-setting rung + contamination-comparison surface, reported with CIs and as a sub-score, never as the standalone axis

BBEH-mini 460-item (Apache-2.0 + CC-BY) — add as the optional HARD-REASONING / frontier-stretch rung (restores the frontier discrimination saturated BBH lost; contamination-resistant) with normalization, accepting that it floors sub-30B local models

Design move: replace the single saturated MMLU-Pro axis with a 2-3 RUNG difficulty ladder scored as a stratified PROFILE (per-rung + worst-rung) with bootstrap CIs, mirroring the existing private-genmath sentinel with a small private hold-out per rung as the contamination canary

EXCLUDE from the runnable suite (cited published context only): GPQA (gated + no-republication), HLE (small-model floor <5% + LLM-judge + ~30% answer noise), DROP (saturated + CC-BY-SA + fragile F1), ARC-AGI-1/2 (grid-JSON OOD + needs program-search; defer to an opt-in abstract-reasoning module)

MuSR (CC-BY) = optional minor narrative-reasoning rung only; SimpleQA-Verified (MIT/CC) = candidate for a SEPARATE future factuality/hallucination axis, both gated behind the LLM-judge / small-size caveats

axis_summary:
Headline: the v0 problem (everyone near ceiling) is REAL and worse than feared for math — across 2025-26 the entire class of FINAL-ANSWER competition math saturated at the frontier. MathArena itself published "Farewell to Final-Answer Competition Problems as Frontier Benchmarks" and reports Apex v2 has ZERO qualifying problems under its "top models do poorly" criterion. AIME 2026 already averages 0.838 with the top dozen models at 93-97% (https://benchlm.ai/math, https://matharena.ai/no_final_answer/). GSM8K (~99%) and MATH-500 (~96%) are fully saturated; MGSM (~92%) is saturated and additionally confounds language with math. So no single math set spans small-local -> frontier. The fix is a 2-3 tier construction, not one bench:

(A) MID/HARD tier that separates LOCAL models from each other and from frontier: fresh competition final-answer sets (HMMT Feb, AIME, BRUMO). These have a large dynamic range over the population we actually serve: a non-reasoning base 7-14B floors near 0 (Qwen2.5-7B = 0.62% on AIME2025) while a reasoning-distilled 7B hits ~53% and frontier ~90%+ (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.16891, https://intuitionlabs.ai/articles/aime-2025-ai-benchmark-explained). That spread is exactly what we lack today. Source the TEXT from MathArena's HuggingFace mirrors (CC-BY-NC-SA) and FRESHNESS-WINDOW for contamination control (eval only the newest cycle, rotate as old sets contaminate — AIME 2024 is provably contaminated).

(B) FRONTIER-DISCRIMINATING tier (only if we want to keep frontier anchors separated): MathArena Apex is the one genuinely unsaturated final-answer set (Gemini 3 Pro = 23.4% SOTA, most models <6%; https://llm-stats.com/benchmarks/matharena-apex). But it FLOORS small local models at 0 (no discrimination among our core users) and the canonical-answer text is gated behind LLM-judge infra. Use it as an anchor-only stretch axis, not a local-user axis. FrontierMath is even more extreme (frontier ~52%, gated verifiers sold to OpenAI) — exclude for local-run, cite as context only.

(C) CONTAMINATION-RESISTANT GENERATED tier (our current approach): keep it but reposition. GSM-Symbolic-style templating (Apple, Apache-2.0, data+templates released) is the right pattern and we should mine its hardening levers: GSM-Symbolic-P2 ("increased complexity") and inserted-irrelevant-info ("NoOp") variants crater models up to 65% and widen model spread far more than the easy base templates (https://machinelearning.apple.com/research/gsm-symbolic). LESSON for our generated axis: easy templated arithmetic saturates (that's why ours doesn't discriminate); difficulty must come from (i) more required reasoning hops, (ii) distractor/irrelevant clauses, (iii) larger/compositional numeric ranges, (iv) symbolic answers needing equivalence-checking. Literature caveat: perturbation alone doesn't fully kill contamination because the underlying reasoning pattern is reused (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.04872 / 2503.16402). So generated math is great for contamination-resistance + a tunable local-band axis, but cannot reach frontier-discriminating hardness by itself — it complements, not replaces, fresh-competition sets.

Licensing landmine to flag loudly: the original Hendrycks MATH (and by extension MATH-500, AIME content scraped from AoPS) was DMCA'd off HuggingFace by Art of Problem Solving in Jan 2025 (https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals/issues/193). The repo's "MIT license" covers CODE only — the problem CONTENT is contested copyright we should NOT redistribute. AIME problems are MAA exam content (historically unenforced, but AoPS forum reproductions are actively DMCA'd). Therefore: do NOT self-host MATH-500/raw-AIME text; if we use competition math, pull from MathArena's explicitly-licensed CC-BY-NC-SA mirrors with attribution (note NC clause vs our servable-data constraint — workable since we're non-commercial/community but must be checked), or generate our own.

Scorer robustness: final-answer numeric/short-form extraction is the stable path (boxed-answer + math-verify/sympy equivalence). Proof grading (USAMO/IMO/Putnam) needs dual-LLM-judge against rubrics — NOT local-runnable, exclude. Omni-MATH/OlympiadBench mix numeric+symbolic+proof; restrict to their auto-gradable open-ended numeric subsets (e.g. OlympiadBench OE_TO_maths_en = 674 text-only problems, Apache-2.0) and skip multimodal/proof items entirely.

benchmarks.name: AIME 2024 / 2025 / 2026 (American Invitational Mathematics Examination)
