benchmarks.license: MAA exam content; NO clean redistributable license. MAA has historically not enforced IP (AoPS forum reproductions), but AoPS DMCA'd the Hendrycks MATH set that contained such problems in Jan 2025. Community HF mirrors exist (HuggingFaceH4/aime_2024, AI-MO/aimo-validation-aime, MathArena/aime_2025|2026) but legality is contested. MathArena's mirror is tagged CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Each year is only 15-30 integer-answer (0-999) problems. Trivially served as plain text; answer is an integer so scoring is exact-match with no parser fragility. No sandbox/tools/images. Runs over any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - AIME 2025/2026 top models cluster 88-97%; AIME 2026 average across 12 evaluated models = 0.838, top (Step-3.5-Flash) 96.67%, Kimi K2.6 96.4% (https://benchlm.ai/math). AIME is now a frontier CEILING, no longer separates strong models.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Strong FLOOR effect for non-reasoning models: base Qwen2.5-7B = 0.62% on AIME 2025; but reasoning-distilled small models recover hugely (OpenThoughts3-7B ~53%), so it discriminates well across the REASONING-TUNED local band, just not for base/small non-reasoning models (https://intuitionlabs.ai/articles/aime-2025-ai-benchmark-explained, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.16891).
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high for 2024 (MathArena found 'strong signs of contamination in AIME 2024'); low for the newest cycle IF evaluated promptly after release. Older sets contaminate as they age into training corpora. Mitigation = freshness windowing + rotation.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no - integer answer, exact match.
benchmarks.data_openness: partial - widely mirrored but copyright-contested; not cleanly ours to redistribute.
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Adopt only the FRESHEST cycle, freshness-windowed, sourced from MathArena's CC-BY-NC-SA mirror with attribution; good for the upper-local-to-strong band but treat as saturating at frontier. Do NOT self-host raw MAA text without the licensed mirror. Pair with a harder tier (Apex/HMMT) for frontier separation.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Olympiad-qualifier-level multi-step math reasoning, integer final answer.
benchmarks.notes: Cheap, clean-scoring, high name recognition, but the single most saturated frontier math signal of 2026. Its value to US is the LOCAL-band spread (reasoning vs non-reasoning), not frontier ranking.

benchmarks.name: MATH (Hendrycks et al., NeurIPS 2021) and MATH-500 subset
benchmarks.license: CODE is MIT (github.com/hendrycks/math/blob/main/LICENSE). DATA/CONTENT is the problem: scraped from AMC/AIME/AoPS and was DMCA'd off HuggingFace by Art of Problem Solving in Jan 2025. Do NOT treat as redistributable.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Text problems, boxed final answer. MATH-500 = 500 items, light to run. Needs a symbolic-equivalence checker (sympy / math-verify) because answers are expressions, not just integers - some answer-extraction fragility but well-solved by existing verifiers.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - frontier ~96% (GPT-5.3 Codex 96% per BenchLM); 'effectively saturated' for top-tier comparison. Best models were ~7% at release in 2021, now >90% (https://artificialanalysis.ai/evaluations/math-500, https://www.lxt.ai/blog/llm-benchmarks/).
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Retains some mid-band signal: spans 5 difficulty levels, so weak local models still separate on Level 4-5, but the easy levels are floored-from-above (everyone gets them). Less floored than AIME for small models, but the ceiling is the killer.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high - in every major pretraining corpus for 4+ years; one of the most contaminated math sets in existence. GSM-Symbolic-style analysis shows GSM8K/MATH scores partly reflect memorization.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no - but requires symbolic equivalence checking (sympy), not raw string match.
benchmarks.data_openness: partial - copyright-contested, removed from HF; mirrors of dubious legality remain.
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Exclude as a discriminator: saturated at frontier AND legally encumbered (DMCA'd). Its only residual use is as a difficulty-calibration reference. If a math-fundamentals floor item is wanted, prefer GSM-Symbolic (clean license) over MATH.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Competition math across 7 subjects x 5 difficulty levels; symbolic/expression final answers.
benchmarks.notes: The DMCA takedown is the load-bearing fact: the 'MIT license' people cite is the harness license, not a content license. Do not redistribute.

benchmarks.name: GSM8K (Grade School Math 8K)
benchmarks.license: MIT (OpenAI). Cleanly redistributable.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Plain-text word problems, single integer/number answer, exact-match scoring (strip #### delimiter). The cleanest-to-run math set there is. Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - frontier ~99%; 'useless for top-tier comparisons,' fully saturated since late 2024 (https://www.lxt.ai/blog/llm-benchmarks/).
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Increasingly saturated even for small models: modern reasoning 7B+ models routinely 85-95%. Only the weakest/oldest tiny models score low. Limited floor signal left for the models our users run.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high - removing contaminated examples dropped some models' accuracy up to 13% (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2311.04850); also the GSM-Symbolic motivating finding.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no - numeric exact match.
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Exclude as a discriminator (project goal). Saturated top-to-mid and contaminated. Keep ONLY as a trivial sanity/smoke check that an endpoint works, never as a scored axis. Its successor for our purpose is GSM-Symbolic.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Grade-school multi-step arithmetic word problems.
benchmarks.notes: The canonical example of a saturated+contaminated benchmark. Useful only as the 'floor' reference everyone knows.

benchmarks.name: MGSM (Multilingual Grade School Math)
benchmarks.license: Source problems = GSM8K (MIT). Translations released by Google Research (google-research-datasets/MGSM, MGSM-Rev2) under permissive terms (Apache/CC-style). Generally redistributable, verify per-file.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 250 problems x 10 languages + English, numeric answer, exact match. Light and endpoint-friendly. No tools/sandbox.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - leader Llama 4 Maverick 0.923, o3-mini 0.920, several >91% (https://llm-stats.com/benchmarks/mgsm). Built on saturated GSM8K problems.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Confounded: low scores on small models reflect missing MULTILINGUAL coverage as much as math ability, so it does not cleanly measure math discrimination. Tiny/English-centric models floor for the wrong reason.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high - inherits GSM8K contamination; the problems are old and translated copies of an already-leaked set.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no.
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Exclude from the MATH axis (it is really a multilingual axis on saturated math). If multilingual is added as its own axis later, MGSM is a candidate THERE, not here. As a math discriminator it fails on saturation + confound.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Grade-school math reasoning across 11 languages (chain-of-thought transfer).
benchmarks.notes: Belongs to a multilingual axis, not math. Listed here only because it was in scope.

benchmarks.name: Omni-MATH
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (dataset card KbsdJames/Omni-MATH and GitHub). Cleanly redistributable - notable advantage vs MATH/AIME.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 4,428 olympiad problems are downloadable text. BUT official scoring uses a GPT-4o judge or the Omni-Judge 7B model (Llama-3.1-8B fine-tune) because answers include symbolic/proof/existence forms. A local user CAN run Omni-Judge locally (it is an 8B model) but that doubles the model footprint and adds judge-noise; restricting to the numeric-answer subset + sympy is the clean path.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating-to-partial at the olympiad level: o1-mini 60.5%, o1-preview 52.6% at release; harder than MATH. Newer frontier models will be higher now but it is far from saturated vs GSM8K/MATH. Good headroom for strong models.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: FLOOR risk: best OPEN model at release was only 36.2%, so sub-30B local models will score low and bunch near the floor on the hard subset - limited discrimination among small local models, but the 10 difficulty tiers let you select an easier band.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: medium - aggregated from public olympiad sources (some old, thus partially in corpora), but breadth (33 subdomains) and the long tail of obscure problems dilute memorization. Not freshness-windowed, so contamination grows over time.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no - but needs a judge/equivalence step (LLM judge or sympy on the numeric subset).
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Best LICENSE of the olympiad sets (Apache-2.0) - attractive for redistribution. Adopt a curated NUMERIC-ANSWER, difficulty-tiered subset scored with sympy (drop proof/existence items and the GPT-4o judge). Use the mid tiers to discriminate the local band; the hard tier for strong anchors. Verify the chosen items aren't AoPS-DMCA-sensitive.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Olympiad-level math across 33 subdomains, 10 difficulty levels; mixed answer types.
benchmarks.notes: The clean-license olympiad option. The catch is judge dependence; solvable by subsetting to numeric answers. Strong candidate for a self-hostable HARD-ish tier.

benchmarks.name: OlympiadBench
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (Hothan/OlympiadBench, OpenBMB GitHub). Cleanly redistributable.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: ~8,476 problems total but 57% are MULTIMODAL (diagrams) - NOT runnable on text endpoints. The text-only open-ended math subset OE_TO_maths_en_COMP (~674 problems) IS local-runnable as text with numeric/symbolic answers + automated scorer. Must explicitly filter to OE_TO and drop multimodal+proof items.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - extremely hard at release (best model GPT-4V averaged 17.97%). Even with 2026 frontier gains this remains a high-headroom set, especially physics. Far from saturated.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: FLOOR risk: brutal difficulty means small local models score very low and bunch near zero - poor discrimination among sub-30B models. Select the easier text-only math items to get usable local-band spread.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: medium - sourced from public olympiads + Chinese college entrance exam; older content partially in corpora, but the multimodal/bilingual long tail and obscurity reduce direct overlap. No freshness windowing.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no for the OE_TO math subset (numeric/symbolic answer + provided scorer); proof items would need a judge (exclude them).
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Adopt ONLY the text-only open-ended English math subset (OE_TO_maths_en, ~674 items, Apache-2.0) with its automated numeric/symbolic scorer; discard multimodal (57%) and proof items. Good clean-license source for a hard math tier; floors small models, so tier-select.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Olympiad-level math (and physics) reasoning; bilingual; mostly multimodal but with a usable text-only math slice.
benchmarks.notes: Headline 8.4k count is misleading for us - the local-usable, text-only English math core is ~674 items. Apache-2.0 is the draw.

benchmarks.name: MathArena (fresh-competition platform: AIME/HMMT/BRUMO/SMT/CMIMC + Apex; proof comps USAMO/IMO/Putnam)
benchmarks.license: Harness CODE = MIT (github.com/eth-sri/matharena). Per-competition DATASETS = CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0 (e.g. MathArena/brumo_2025, apex_2025, aime_2026). NC clause: usable for a non-commercial community leaderboard with attribution+sharealike, but flag the NC term against any future commercial framing.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: FINAL-ANSWER comps (AIME/HMMT/BRUMO/SMT/CMIMC/Apex) are local-runnable: text problems, numeric/short answers, auto-graded; harness supports vLLM + OpenAI/together/openrouter etc. (so any OpenAI-compatible endpoint works). PROOF comps (USAMO/IMO/Putnam) need dual-LLM-judge against human rubrics - NOT local-runnable, exclude. We can also just consume the CC-BY-NC-SA answer keys and score with our own runner.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: MIXED and this is the key insight: final-answer comps are NOW SATURATING at the frontier (HMMT Feb 2026 top ~88%, AIME 2026 avg 0.838). MathArena published 'Farewell to Final-Answer Competition Problems as Frontier Benchmarks' and Apex v2 has ZERO qualifying problems. The exception, Apex (v1), is genuinely DISCRIMINATING: Gemini 3 Pro SOTA 23.4%, most models <6% (https://llm-stats.com/benchmarks/matharena-apex, https://matharena.ai/no_final_answer/).
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Final-answer comps discriminate the reasoning-tuned local band (HMMT/AIME spread base-7B ~0 to frontier ~90%). Apex FLOORS all local models near 0 (frontier-only discriminator). So MathArena gives you a tunable difficulty ladder: BRUMO/HMMT for local band, Apex for frontier anchors.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low - the entire design is contamination resistance via evaluating models 'as soon as new problems are released.' They empirically detected AIME 2024 contamination and avoid it by freshness. Best-in-class contamination posture of all math options. Requires ongoing rotation as each cycle ages.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no for final-answer comps (numeric/short-answer equivalence); proof comps need LLM judges (exclude).
benchmarks.data_openness: open (CC-BY-NC-SA mirrors on HuggingFace under MathArena org).
used_by: model cards now cite MathArena AIME/HMMT/Apex results (Qwen, GLM, DeepSeek, Kimi)
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Primary recommended math source. Use its CC-BY-NC-SA fresh final-answer sets (newest HMMT Feb + AIME + BRUMO) as the contamination-resistant LOCAL->strong discriminator, freshness-windowed/rotated, with attribution. Optionally add Apex as an ANCHOR-ONLY frontier-separation axis (it floors local users, so don't score it for them). Adopt their methodology (eval-on-release, rotate) even for our generated items. Confirm NC clause is compatible with our community/non-commercial distribution.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Uncontaminated, just-released competition math; final-answer reasoning (and proof, which we skip).
benchmarks.notes: The strategic winner: gives BOTH a clean-licensed contamination-resistant local-band set (HMMT/BRUMO/AIME mirrors) AND a frontier-discriminating stretch set (Apex). The methodology (eval-on-release + rotate) is the template our whole math axis should copy.

benchmarks.name: FrontierMath (Epoch AI, Tiers 1-4)
benchmarks.license: Benchmark described as CC-BY by Epoch for their published materials, BUT the actual problems are private/held-out and the VERIFIERS are sold (only OpenAI has purchased access). Effectively gated; the solvable data is not openly servable.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Problems are unpublished by design (data-protection protocol to prevent contamination); scoring requires Epoch's private automated verifiers. A local user cannot obtain the data or the scorer. Hard exclusion for a local-runnable suite.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - the hardest published math frontier benchmark; as of May 2026 GPT-5.5 Pro leads at 52.4%, GPT-5.5 51.7% (https://benchlm.ai/benchmarks/frontierMath). Tier 4 far lower. Excellent frontier separation.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Total FLOOR - research-mathematician-level problems; every local model scores ~0. Zero discrimination among our users.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low (private, novel, strict protocols) - the gold standard for contamination resistance, but achieved precisely by being unavailable to us.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes (problems often need a sympy/numeric verification harness) and the verifier is proprietary.
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Exclude for local-run (gated data + proprietary verifiers + floors all local models). Cite ONLY as the frontier-ceiling reference point that explains why we anchor against APIs. Do not attempt to source or self-host.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Research-level / exceptionally hard novel mathematics, far beyond competition level.
benchmarks.notes: Important context, not a usable item. A June 12 2026 update corrected ~42% of problems (now 338 total: 295 base + 43 Tier-4), underscoring how hard these are even to author.

benchmarks.name: GSM-Symbolic / GSM-Symbolic-P1 / -P2 (Apple) - templated/generated math, the model for OUR generated axis
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (apple/ml-gsm-symbolic, apple/GSM-Symbolic on HF). Templates AND generated data released. Fully redistributable + we can generate our own from the templates.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Generated word problems, numeric answer, exact match. Templates let us synthesize unlimited fresh instances locally - ideal for a self-hosted, no-gated-data axis on any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial/tunable - the BASE GSM-Symbolic is easy (frontier still high), but the HARD variants discriminate: GSM-Symbolic-P2 (more reasoning hops) and the inserted-irrelevant-info 'NoOp' variant cause drops up to 65% and widen model spread substantially. Difficulty is a DIAL, not fixed (https://machinelearning.apple.com/research/gsm-symbolic).
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Good local-band discrimination when hardness is dialed up: small models degrade much more than frontier under P2/NoOp (e.g. Mistral-7B -9.2% vs GPT-4o -0.3% even on mild perturbation), so the spread grows exactly where we need it.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: LOW by construction - fresh instances per run via templating + randomized values/names/distractors; the headline use-case is detecting/avoiding GSM8K contamination. Caveat from literature: perturbation reuses the same reasoning pattern, so it is not immune if the TEMPLATE family is itself memorized - mitigate with novel templates + private sentinel set (which the project already split out).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no - numeric exact match; generation is offline templating.
used_by: widely cited as the contamination/robustness probe
benchmarks.recommendation_note: Keep and EXTEND our generated-math axis using GSM-Symbolic's hardening levers - this is the fix for 'our generated math doesn't discriminate.' Difficulty must come from: (i) more reasoning hops (P2-style), (ii) irrelevant/distractor clauses (NoOp), (iii) larger/compositional numeric ranges, (iv) symbolic answers w/ sympy equivalence. Caveat: generated math alone CANNOT reach frontier-discriminating hardness (it tops out around hard-grade-school/early-competition) - it complements fresh-competition sets, it does not replace them. Excellent for contamination-resistance + a tunable LOCAL-band axis + a private sentinel.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Robustness of multi-step arithmetic reasoning under symbolic perturbation, distractors, and increased complexity.
benchmarks.notes: Directly answers the project's 'can generated math be made hard enough?' question: hard ENOUGH to discriminate the local band and resist contamination - NOT hard enough to separate frontier models (use HMMT/Apex for that). Pattern to copy: parameterize values+names+distractors+hop-count, score numerically, keep a private sentinel split.

top_picks_for_local_suite: PRIMARY (contamination-resistant, local-band discriminator): MathArena FRESH FINAL-ANSWER sets - newest HMMT Feb + AIME + BRUMO cycle, sourced from MathArena's CC-BY-NC-SA HuggingFace mirrors with attribution, FRESHNESS-WINDOWED and rotated each cycle (copy their eval-on-release methodology). Scored by exact integer / sympy equivalence. This is the cleanest path to a math axis that separates non-reasoning -> reasoning-tuned -> strong local models. (Confirm NC clause OK for our community distribution.)

GENERATED AXIS (keep + harden, fully self-hostable, Apache-2.0): extend our current templated-math axis using GSM-Symbolic's difficulty levers - more reasoning hops (P2), inserted irrelevant/distractor clauses (NoOp), larger/compositional numeric ranges, and symbolic answers checked with sympy. Maintain the public set + private sentinel split (already done in task #16). Fixes the 'generated math doesn't discriminate' problem for the LOCAL band and gives contamination immunity; does NOT reach frontier hardness on its own.

HARD TIER for strong-local + anchor headroom (clean license): a curated NUMERIC-ANSWER, difficulty-tiered subset of Omni-MATH (Apache-2.0) and/or OlympiadBench OE_TO_maths_en (Apache-2.0, ~674 text-only items) - drop proof/multimodal/existence items, score with sympy, select mid tiers so small local models aren't fully floored. These are the self-hostable way to add headroom above HMMT for strong local models.

ANCHOR-ONLY frontier separation (optional, do NOT score for local users): MathArena Apex as a stretch axis run only against API anchors (Gemini 3 Pro SOTA 23.4%, most <6%) - it floors all local models near 0, so it discriminates frontier anchors but is meaningless for our core users; surface it as an 'anchor stretch' metric only.

EXCLUDE as scored discriminators: GSM8K, MGSM, MATH-500 (all saturated top-to-mid AND/OR contaminated; MATH-500 additionally DMCA-encumbered). Keep GSM8K only as a one-item endpoint sanity check. EXCLUDE FrontierMath (gated data + proprietary verifiers + floors all local models) - cite only as the frontier-ceiling reference that justifies API anchoring.

CROSS-CUTTING RULES: (1) Never self-host raw Hendrycks-MATH or raw AIME text - DMCA'd / contested; use MathArena's licensed mirrors or generate our own. (2) Build the math axis as a DIFFICULTY LADDER (generated -> HMMT/BRUMO -> Omni/Olympiad hard subset -> Apex anchor-only) so it spans base-7B (~0 on AIME) to frontier (~52% on FrontierMath) instead of one saturated set. (3) Avoid proof grading entirely (needs dual-LLM judges, not local-runnable). (4) Rotate fresh sets on release; keep a private generated sentinel - the only durable defenses as benchmarks contaminate.

axis_summary: The agentic axis is the hardest to make local-runnable, and being blunt is the honest service to the product. The dominant agentic benchmarks (SWE-bench, Terminal-Bench, GAIA, WebArena, tau2-bench, AppWorld, OSWorld) all require some combination of: a Docker sandbox, a live browser/web stack, external paid APIs (SerpAPI, RapidAPI), an LLM user-simulator, and an LLM judge. Two structural problems make these a poor fit for a community leaderboard that asks an ordinary user to run a frozen suite against an OpenAI-compatible endpoint on a single consumer GPU. (1) HARNESS/COST: a single GAIA or SWE-bench run can take an hour-plus and pull gigabytes of Docker images; tau2-bench and GAIA also burn anchor-side API tokens (judge + user-simulator), so cost and run-to-run variance balloon. (2) SAFETY/VALIDITY: Berkeley RDI (Apr 2026) showed eight major agent benchmarks — SWE-bench, Terminal-Bench, WebArena, OSWorld, GAIA among them — can be driven to ~100% WITHOUT solving any task, because the agent executes code in the SAME container the evaluator scores in (pytest conftest hooks, trojaned binaries, file:// reads of gold answers, prompt-injected judges). For a self-reported community leaderboard this is fatal: it is both a cheat surface AND a real risk of running model-generated shell/code on the user's own machine. https://rdi.berkeley.edu/blog/trustworthy-benchmarks-cont/ . CONTAMINATION compounds it: ~94% of SWE-bench Verified issues predate frontier cutoffs and ~1/3 leak the patch verbatim; OpenAI publicly retired SWE-bench Verified as a frontier signal for exactly this reason ( https://openai.com/index/why-we-no-longer-evaluate-swe-bench-verified/ ). THE ONE EXCEPTION — and it is a strong one — is STATIC FUNCTION-CALLING / TOOL-SELECTION, exemplified by BFCL's non-live AST categories. These need only a model endpoint (no sandbox, no external API, no code execution, no LLM judge — pure AST comparison against a gold function call), are Apache-2.0 and fully redistributable, and DISCRIMINATE CLEANLY across the whole local-to-frontier range with no floor effect for small models (Qwen3.5 2B 43.6% -> 4B 50.3% -> 9B 66.1% -> Llama-3.1-8B 76.1% -> 70B 84.8% -> 405B 88.5%) and no saturation at the top. RECOMMENDATION: adopt a BFCL-derived static tool-use axis (vendor the AST single-turn categories + relevance/irrelevance only) as the core agentic signal that ships in the default local suite; defer EVERYTHING that needs a sandbox/browser/external API to an explicit, opt-in "Docker module" run by power users, clearly flagged as higher-variance, anchor-cost-bearing, and never part of the default self-serve flow. tau2-bench is the best candidate for that opt-in module (MIT, OpenAI-compatible, well-discriminating, no user-hostile Docker — but needs API-side user-simulator+judge tokens). Treat all SWE/Terminal/Web/GAIA numbers as cite-the-published-leaderboard context for anchors, not as something a local user reproduces.
top_picks_for_local_suite: BFCL (Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard) — NON-LIVE AST SUBSET ONLY (single-turn simple/multiple/parallel AST in Python/Java/JS + relevance/irrelevance). This is the ONLY agentic benchmark that is genuinely default-local-runnable: Apache-2.0/redistributable, needs only an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (no sandbox, no external API, no code-exec, no LLM judge — deterministic AST match to a gold call), and discriminates across the full local-to-frontier range with no small-model floor and no frontier saturation. Adopt as the core agentic axis. Vendor the data + AST scorer; EXCLUDE the live/executable and multi-turn-stateful categories (they need API backends).

tau2-bench — RECOMMENDED ONLY AS OPT-IN MODULE, not default. MIT-licensed, OpenAI-compatible agent+user LLMs, strong discrimination (esp. telecom: pass^1 collapses for weaker models). No user-hostile Docker, but REQUIRES anchor/API-side tokens for the LLM user-simulator AND LLM judge, so it bears cost + run-to-run variance unsuitable for the zero-friction default flow. Offer behind an explicit toggle for users who accept the token cost.

Everything else (SWE-bench Verified/Lite/Pro, Terminal-Bench 2.0, GAIA, WebArena/VisualWebArena, AppWorld, OSWorld, BrowseComp, ToolBench) — DEFER to a clearly-flagged Docker-only opt-in module or use published-leaderboard numbers as anchor context. None are safe or practical as default self-serve local runs: each needs a sandbox/live-browser/external-API, and all are exposed by the Berkeley RDI same-container cheat/safety finding.

benchmarks.name: BFCL (Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard) v3/v4
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (dataset + eval code + leaderboard stats all under Apache-2.0). Installable as `bfcl-eval` (PyPI) or from the Gorilla repo.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: YES for the non-live AST subset (single-turn simple/multiple/parallel AST + relevance/irrelevance): needs ONLY an OpenAI-compatible model endpoint; scoring is deterministic AST comparison of the emitted function call against a gold call — no sandbox, no external API, no code execution, no LLM judge. This subset is the single best agentic fit for the default local suite. NO for the rest: 'Live' executable categories call real/mock API backends, 'web_search' needs SerpAPI (external paid key), and multi-turn categories are stateful (execute against a backend to compare end state). Recommendation: vendor + ship the AST subset; drop live/multi-turn.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating — not saturated. Frontier/large still leaves clear headroom and ordering: Llama-3.1-405B 88.5%, 70B 84.8% on the overall metric; BFCL-v3 multi-turn leader GLM-4.5 ~76.7% (Jun 2026). Frontier does NOT hit ~100% on the composite. Sources: https://gorilla.cs.berkeley.edu/leaderboard.html , https://llm-stats.com/benchmarks/bfcl-v3
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: NO floor effect on the AST subset — clean monotonic spread across the local range: Qwen3.5 2B 43.6% -> 4B 50.3% -> 9B 66.1% (BFCL-v4, Qwen-reported); Llama-3.1-8B 76.1%. (Multi-turn DOES floor tiny models — Qwen3-0.6B ~1.4%, xLAM-1B ~8% — another reason to ship the single-turn AST subset, not multi-turn.) Sources: https://github.com/QwenLM/Qwen3-Coder (SUPPORTED_MODELS / BFCL results), https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.22138 (TinyLLM)
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — categories are partly synthetic/templated and the dataset is public on HF, so memorization of specific items is possible; mitigated by BFCL's periodic version bumps (v1->v4) and by AST checking parameter structure rather than a memorizable string answer. A private regenerated/held-out variant in our harness would harden it further.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no (for the AST subset) — AST parse + structural compare only. yes for the 'Live'/executable and multi-turn-state categories, which we exclude.
used_by: widely cited as the standard function-calling leaderboard
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Whether a model emits correct, well-formed tool/function calls (right function, right parameters, right types; and correctly abstains when no function is relevant) given a tool schema and a user request.
benchmarks.notes: This is the keystone recommendation for the axis. Ship the non-live single-turn AST categories + relevance/irrelevance as the default 'tool-use' axis. Pin a specific BFCL version commit in the manifest (categories/counts shift between versions). ~1,390 non-live AST entries available to sample from; subset to a fixed item-set for the frozen suite. Answer-extraction fragility is the main engineering risk — models emit tool calls in many shapes (OpenAI tool_calls vs inline JSON vs Python) so the parser must normalize formats before AST compare; budget parity-testing against the official bfcl-eval extractor.

benchmarks.name: Gorilla (APIBench)
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (Gorilla repo). Underlying API docs (HuggingFace/TorchHub/TensorHub model hubs) scraped.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: The original Gorilla/APIBench (predecessor to BFCL) evaluates API-call generation against 3 ML model-hub API sets using AST sub-tree matching — that core check needs only a model endpoint and is local-runnable. BUT it is effectively superseded by BFCL (same authors), is narrower (ML-hub APIs only), and is now dated. No reason to adopt Gorilla separately if we adopt the BFCL AST subset.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial — older/narrow; modern frontier models do very well on the 3 hub APIs, so it has limited frontier headroom relative to BFCL. Treat as historical.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Not separately characterized in current data; subsumed by BFCL findings.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high — 2023-era, widely mirrored, ML-hub API docs are ubiquitous in training data; high memorization risk.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no — AST sub-tree match (for the non-executable evaluation mode).
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Generating the correct ML-model-hub API invocation (function + args) for a request, via AST matching.
benchmarks.notes: Folded into BFCL. Mention only as lineage; do not adopt as a separate axis.

benchmarks.name: tau-bench / tau2-bench (Sierra)
benchmarks.license: MIT (sierra-research/tau2-bench).
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: No Docker and no live external website required, and the agent + user-simulator can both point at OpenAI-compatible endpoints (LiteLLM api_base/api_key) — so it is far more tractable than browser/SWE benches. BUT it is NOT a clean default-local benchmark: it REQUIRES an LLM user-simulator AND an LLM-based reward/judge in the loop, which means meaningful API-token cost and run-to-run stochasticity (the user-sim makes the conversation non-deterministic). Best offered as an opt-in module where the user accepts the anchor/API cost; not in the zero-friction default flow.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating — telecom domain in particular is hard and NOT saturated; pass^1 drops sharply for weaker models (gpt-4.1 retail 74% / airline 56% / telecom 34%). pass^k (reliability over k repeats) degrades fast, which is excellent discrimination signal. Telecom leader ~99% exists but only for top reasoning models; the spread below is wide (206 models, mean 46.8, sd 29.8 on Tau2 per llm-stats). Sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.07982 , https://llm-stats.com/benchmarks/tau2-telecom
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Small open models score low but non-zero on retail/airline (partial floor on telecom). Discriminates in the mid-band; some risk of floor for sub-7B on telecom. Source: https://quesma.com/blog/tau2-benchmark-improving-results-smaller-models/
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — tasks/policies are public on GitHub; but success depends on multi-turn dynamic interaction with a stochastic user-sim, which is much harder to memorize than a static QA pair. Moderate.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no host code-exec on the user's machine — tools are simulated Python within the harness process (not arbitrary model-authored shell). Lower safety risk than SWE/Terminal/Web. The cost is API tokens (user-sim + judge), not sandbox risk.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multi-turn tool-agent-user interaction in enterprise domains (retail/airline/telecom): can the agent follow policy docs, call domain tools correctly, and resolve the user's request reliably (pass^k) through a simulated dialogue.
benchmarks.notes: Top pick for an OPT-IN agentic module if we want a dynamic/multi-turn signal beyond static BFCL. Flag clearly: bears anchor-side API cost (user-sim+judge), is stochastic (report pass^k with seeds/repeats), and the judge is an attack surface (Berkeley RDI flagged unprotected LLM judges generally). Pin domain + task IDs + user-sim model in the manifest for reproducibility.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Verified
benchmarks.license: Eval harness code MIT (github.com/SWE-bench/SWE-bench, LICENSE.md). DATASET: per-repository — task content derives from the underlying GitHub repos under their own licenses (mixed MIT/Apache/BSD/GPL); the curated 500-task set is distributed on HF for research use. Redistribution of the model-runnable environment is via Dockerfiles, not blanket re-hosting of all repo code. Treat dataset redistribution as 'partial / per-repo, verify before re-hosting'.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Each task runs a real repo's test suite inside a per-task Docker container; the agent must edit code and the harness executes pytest to score. This means: Docker required, multi-GB images, ~hour-plus full runs, and — critically — the agent's generated code executes in the SAME container that computes the score. Berkeley RDI showed this lets a conftest.py hook force all tests green (100% without solving anything). Running arbitrary model-authored patches+tests on a user's own machine is also a real safety risk. Not viable as a default local benchmark.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial/eroding — frontier ~80% on Verified (Opus 4.5 80.9%, Gemini 3.1 Pro 80.6%); OpenAI RETIRED Verified as a frontier signal due to contamination. SWE-bench Pro (harder, 731+ tasks, GPL/copyleft to deter training inclusion) restores discrimination: same top models fall to ~46-57%. Sources: https://epoch.ai/benchmarks/swe-bench-verified , https://openai.com/index/why-we-no-longer-evaluate-swe-bench-verified/ , https://labs.scale.com/leaderboard/swe_bench_pro_public
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Strong floor effect for small local models — sub-30B coding models without a strong agent scaffold resolve very few tasks; scores are dominated by the agent harness as much as the model, so a bare local model via a simple loop would score near the floor and the benchmark would not discriminate among small models. (Harness-confounded.)
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high — ~94% of Verified issues predate frontier cutoffs; ~1/3 leak solution code verbatim in the issue/comments. Mitigations live in successors (SWE-bench Pro copyleft deterrent; SWE-rebench/SWE-bench++ decontaminated pipelines), not in Verified itself. Sources: https://openai.com/index/why-we-no-longer-evaluate-swe-bench-verified/ , https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.20411
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — executes model-authored patches + repo test suites in Docker. Same-container cheat + host-safety risk.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Resolving real GitHub issues (produce a patch that passes the repo's hidden tests) — autonomous SWE capability.
benchmarks.notes: Use ONLY as published-leaderboard anchor context, never as a local run. If a Docker opt-in module is ever built, prefer SWE-bench Pro (better contamination posture + discrimination) or a decontaminated, date-windowed pipeline (SWE-rebench-style), and isolate the agent from the evaluator container to defeat the conftest cheat.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Lite
benchmarks.license: Same as SWE-bench (harness MIT; dataset per-repo). Lite = 300-task cheaper subset.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Same Docker + in-container code-execution + same-container-cheat + host-safety profile as Verified, just fewer tasks. Cheaper, but none of the structural blockers change. Not a default local benchmark.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial — easier subset, so MORE saturated/leakier than Verified at the top; weak discrimination at frontier.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floor effect for small models, same as Verified.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high — subset of the same contaminated pool; Lite was specifically noted as having higher solution-leakage rates.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — same as Verified.
used_by: coding-agent projects wanting a cheaper SWE-bench
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Same as SWE-bench Verified on a 300-task subset.
benchmarks.notes: No advantage over Verified for our purposes; both excluded from local runs.

benchmarks.name: Terminal-Bench (2.0 / Hard)
benchmarks.license: Code on GitHub (harbor-framework/terminal-bench). License not clearly stated in current public sources — FLAG as unverified; tasks bundle third-party software in Docker images with their own licenses. Verify before any redistribution.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Each of the 89 tasks runs in a per-task Docker container; the Harbor harness drives the model to issue shell commands that execute in that container, then runs verification scripts inside the same environment. Berkeley RDI demonstrated trojaned-binary cheats (replace curl/uvx to fake passing output) — same-container validity failure — plus the obvious risk of running model-authored shell on the user's box. Docker-heavy, slow. Not a default local benchmark.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating (good) — explicitly built so 'no model can finish' all tasks: Gemini 3.1 Pro 78.4%, GPT-5.3-Codex 77.3%, Opus 4.6 74.7% on 2.0; Terminal-Bench Hard far lower (~60% top). Strong frontier headroom. Sources: https://www.tbench.ai/leaderboard/terminal-bench/2.0 , https://artificialanalysis.ai/evaluations/terminalbench-hard
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Severe floor for small local models — these multi-step shell/SWE/security tasks are out of reach for bare sub-30B models without a strong agent scaffold; near-floor scores, poor discrimination among small models.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med-high — tasks are public on GitHub; mitigated somewhat by complexity, but the 2.0 task set is now widely referenced.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — executes model-authored shell in Docker. Same-container cheat + host-safety risk.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Completing complex multi-step tasks in a real terminal/shell environment (build/configure/exploit), graded by programmatic verification.
benchmarks.notes: Anchor-context only. If ever offered as a Docker opt-in, MUST isolate the verifier from the agent container (Berkeley RDI) and warn loudly about executing model shell locally. Confirm license before touching the data.

benchmarks.name: GAIA
benchmarks.license: Dataset CC-BY-4.0 (gaia-benchmark on HF) — redistributable WITH attribution, which is favorable. Validation answers are public; test answers held on a gated leaderboard.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: GAIA tasks require live web browsing, file parsing, and multi-tool use to reach a short ground-truth answer — so a faithful run needs a browser/search stack and tool orchestration, not just a model endpoint. Scores are also hugely harness-dependent (same task: 44.8% bare GPT-5-mini vs 74.6% with a strong scaffold), so a bare-model local run is not comparable across users. Berkeley RDI also showed GAIA's public HF answers + aggressive string-normalization enable trivial gaming. Not a default local benchmark.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating (good) — top agents ~75% (HAL Sonnet 4.5 74.6%); plenty of headroom and wide spread. The validation leaderboard was closed as 'no longer informative,' but the full task set still discriminates at frontier. Sources: https://hal.cs.princeton.edu/gaia , https://huggingface.co/spaces/gaia-benchmark/leaderboard
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Strong floor for small local models without tools/scaffold — a bare local model with no browser cannot do multi-hop web tasks; near-floor, no discrimination. Score is dominated by the agent framework.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high (as scored naively) — validation ground-truth is public on HF and string-match normalization collides; Berkeley RDI flagged both. Faithful test-set scoring needs the gated leaderboard.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no host code-exec required per se, but requires live browser/web + external search/tool calls — equivalent external-dependency burden, and the browser tool is itself an attack/cheat surface (file:// reads).
benchmarks.what_it_measures: General AI-assistant capability: multi-step reasoning + web browsing + multimodal + tool-use to produce a single verifiable short answer.
benchmarks.notes: CC-BY data is the one bright spot (we could re-host), but the live-browser dependency + harness-dominated scores + public-answer gaming rule it out as a local self-serve run. Anchor-context only, or a heavily-scaffolded Docker opt-in.

benchmarks.name: WebArena
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (web-arena-x/webarena code). Self-host websites via provided Docker images + AMI.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Requires standing up a full self-hosted web stack (multiple Dockerized sites: shopping, GitLab, Reddit clone, CMS, maps) and driving a live browser agent against it. Heavy infra, slow, browser-automation dependency. Berkeley RDI showed extensive WebArena cheats (file:// reads of task configs with gold answers, hidden-DOM substring spoofing, prompt-injecting the LLM judge). Categorically not a default local benchmark.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating (good) — top ~68-72% (OpAgent 71.6%, Claude Mythos 68.7%); far from saturated, but scores are pipeline/agent-dominated. Sources: https://benchlm.ai/benchmarks/webArena , https://awesomeagents.ai/leaderboards/web-agent-benchmarks-leaderboard/
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Strong floor for small local models — needs robust browser-grounding + long-horizon planning; bare small models near floor, no discrimination.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med (task design) but the validity problem is the cheat surface (Berkeley RDI), not memorization per se.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no arbitrary host code-exec, but requires self-hosted web servers + live browser automation — equivalent or greater infra burden, plus browser-as-attack-surface.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Autonomous web-navigation agents completing realistic tasks on self-hosted websites.
benchmarks.notes: Infra + safety + harness-confound make this unusable for self-serve local. Anchor-context only.

benchmarks.name: VisualWebArena
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (web-arena-x/visualwebarena). Self-host via Docker/AMI.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: All of WebArena's blockers PLUS a vision requirement (multimodal grounding on screenshots) — even heavier, and many local OpenAI-compatible setups are text-only. Not a default local benchmark.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating (very hard) — even strong multimodal agents ~16% baseline; lots of headroom but the difficulty + infra make it impractical here. Source: https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/visualwebarena-vwa
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floor for essentially all local models — extremely hard + needs vision; no discrimination at the small end.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med; same cheat-surface caveats as WebArena.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no host code-exec, but self-hosted web + live browser + vision pipeline.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multimodal web agents completing visually-grounded tasks on self-hosted sites.
benchmarks.notes: Hardest infra + vision requirement; exclude. Anchor-context only.

benchmarks.name: BrowseComp (OpenAI)
benchmarks.license: Open-source benchmark released by OpenAI (Apr 2025); questions+reference answers public. Grading uses an LLM judge (HLE-style prompt). Confirm exact license tag before redistribution — FLAG as 'open but verify license string'.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: By construction the 1,266 questions are UNSOLVABLE without live, persistent multi-hop web browsing (GPT-4.5 without browsing ~0.9%). So a faithful run needs a real browsing agent + external web access AND an LLM judge for grading. Not a default local benchmark (a local model with no browser scores ~floor). Useful only to characterize browsing agents.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating (good) — wide spread: purpose-built Deep Research 51.5%, o3 49.7%, up to GPT-5.5 Pro ~90% with strong browsing; bare models near 0%. Strong separation, but entirely browsing-stack-dependent. Source: https://llm-stats.com/benchmarks/browsecomp , https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/.../browsecomp.pdf
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floor at ~0% for any model without a browsing tool — measures the browsing stack as much as the model; no discrimination for bare local models.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — answers public, but tasks are deliberately obscure/hard-to-find; the difficulty is in retrieval, not recall. The LLM judge is an attack surface.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no host code-exec, but mandatory live web browsing + external search + LLM judge.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Hard multi-hop web-browsing / deep-research retrieval to find a short hard-to-locate fact.
benchmarks.notes: Measures browsing infrastructure, not local model intelligence in isolation. Anchor-context only.

benchmarks.name: AppWorld
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (StonyBrookNLP/appworld — framework + task data). Favorable for redistribution.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Better than browser/SWE benches: the 'world' is a self-contained simulated app ecosystem (9 apps, 457 APIs) that runs locally — no live internet, no external paid API. BUT it is an INTERACTIVE CODING agent benchmark: the model writes and EXECUTES Python against the AppWorld engine to complete tasks, with state-based checking. That means running model-authored code in the harness (a sandbox/exec surface) and a non-trivial multi-step orchestration loop — heavier than a static eval and carrying the same same-environment-execution caution. Feasible as a Docker/opt-in module for power users; NOT a zero-friction default local run.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating (good) — hard: GPT-4o ~49% normal / ~30% challenge; best RL agents ~71% TGC. Clear frontier headroom. Sources: https://appworld.dev/leaderboard , https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01861
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Partial floor — small models struggle with multi-step interactive code + state, but RL-tuned mid-size (Qwen2.5-32B+LOOP) reaches ~71%, so there is mid-band signal; bare small models score low.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — Apache data is public; interactive/stateful execution is harder to memorize than static QA, but task scenarios are now public.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — model writes+executes Python in the AppWorld engine; sandboxed but still code-exec. Lower external-dependency risk than SWE (no internet), but same host-exec caution.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Interactive coding agents: write+run code calling simulated app APIs to complete day-to-day digital tasks for simulated people, with state-based correctness checks.
benchmarks.notes: Best of the 'heavy' agentic benches for a self-contained Docker opt-in (Apache-2.0, no live internet/paid API). If we ever build a code-exec module, AppWorld is a stronger, safer-than-SWE candidate. Still exclude from default local flow due to code execution + multi-step harness.

benchmarks.name: ToolBench / ToolLLM (OpenBMB)
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (OpenBMB/ToolBench). StableToolBench (THUNLP-MT) also Apache-2.0.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Original ToolBench depends on 16k+ live RapidAPI APIs — only ~44% of calls succeed (expired keys, network, auth), so it is non-reproducible and needs an external RapidAPI key. StableToolBench patches this with a virtual/cached API server + LLM-simulated API responses, which removes the live dependency but introduces an LLM-simulator + LLM judge (cost + stochasticity) and is heavier infra. Neither is a clean default local run. Superseded for our purpose by BFCL's static AST subset.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial/unreliable — instability and LLM-judge scoring make frontier comparisons noisy; not a dependable discriminator. Source: https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.07714
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Hard to characterize due to instability; not recommended as a discriminator.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high — 2023-era, widely mirrored; plus the live-API instability dominates the signal.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no host code-exec, but needs live RapidAPI (orig) OR an LLM API-simulator + judge (StableToolBench).
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multi-step real-world API/tool use across thousands of RapidAPI tools.
benchmarks.notes: Stale + unstable + judge-dependent. BFCL's AST subset is the modern, reproducible replacement. Exclude.

benchmarks.name: AgentBench (THUDM)
benchmarks.license: Code on GitHub (THUDM/AgentBench). License not clearly stated in current public sources — FLAG as unverified; 8 sub-environments bundle heterogeneous third-party components with their own licenses.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Aggregates 8 heterogeneous environments (OS shell, DB, knowledge-graph, card game, web browsing, etc.), several requiring Docker, a live DB/KG backend, or a browser. Multi-environment orchestration is heavy and the OS/web parts carry the same model-executes-actions safety/cheat caveats. Not a default local benchmark; also somewhat dated (ICLR'24).
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial — 2023/24-era; modern frontier models have moved well past several sub-tasks, so discrimination at the top is weaker than purpose-built 2026 benches.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Mixed by environment; OSS models historically far below commercial — some discrimination, but confounded by per-environment harness differences.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high — widely mirrored since 2023; several sub-tasks memorizable.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes for OS/code sub-environments (shell exec); plus external DB/KG/browser backends.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: LLM-as-agent across 8 diverse environments (reasoning + decision-making + instruction-following).
benchmarks.notes: Too heterogeneous/heavy/dated and partly host-exec. Exclude. Verify license if ever revisited.

benchmarks.name: OSWorld
benchmarks.license: Code/tasks public on GitHub (xlang-ai/OSWorld) — verify exact license before redistribution (FLAG).
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Runs a full GUI virtual machine (real OS desktop) and drives the model to perform computer-use actions; needs a VM/Docker desktop env + screen-grounding. Berkeley RDI showed OSWorld tasks embed public HF gold-file URLs the agent can download to 'self-compare' to 100%. Extremely heavy + vision + same-env cheat. Categorically not a default local benchmark.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating (good) — computer-use is hard; frontier still well below ceiling. But impractical here.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floor for all local models without computer-use scaffolding + vision; no discrimination.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high as scored naively (public gold files; Berkeley RDI).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — full GUI VM + model-driven computer-use actions; heaviest infra + safety surface of all listed.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Computer-use agents performing real desktop GUI tasks across apps in a VM.
benchmarks.notes: Included for completeness (named in 2026 agentic-bench roundups). Anchor-context only; never local.

axis_summary:
CRITICAL FINDING FOR LOCAL-BENCH: Almost every coding benchmark scores by EXECUTING model-generated code against test cases (HumanEval+, MBPP+, BigCodeBench, LiveCodeBench, SciCode, Aider, SWE-bench all do). On unmanaged consumer machines this is a security + support trap, and the benchmark authors agree: EvalPlus itself "strongly recommends Docker" and ships an explicit risk warning for bare-metal runs (https://github.com/evalplus/evalplus/blob/master/docs/execution.md). So the generation-style coding axis is fundamentally a Docker-only opt-in module, exactly as the v0 plan already concluded.

THE ONE SAFE EXCEPTION = exec-free CODE REASONING. CRUXEval-O (output prediction) asks the model to mentally execute a short Python function and write the predicted OUTPUT as text; the gold output already ships in the dataset, so scoring is pure exact-match string comparison with ZERO execution of model code (MIT license, 800 samples, Facebook Research; https://github.com/facebookresearch/cruxeval). This is the only coding benchmark in the catalog that a local user can run safely via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint with no sandbox, no Docker, no untrusted code. (Note: CRUXEval-I / input-prediction does need execution to verify the predicted input reproduces the target output, so adopt only the -O half exec-free.) Discrimination is good but the absolute ceiling is moderate (GPT-4 ~63% in 2024; modern frontier ~80s%), and it measures reasoning-about-code rather than code authorship — so it is a PROXY for coding skill, not a substitute. Recommendation: ship CRUXEval-O as the launch coding axis (safe, simple, contamination-trackable via its fixed set + your generated-variant philosophy), and defer all execution-based benches (LiveCodeBench, HumanEval+/MBPP+, BigCodeBench, SciCode, Aider, SWE-bench) to a clearly-labelled Docker-only opt-in module.

DISCRIMINATION LANDSCAPE (verified 2026-06): HumanEval+/MBPP+ are SATURATED at the frontier (~89-92% pass@1, o1 89%, GPT-4o 90.2%) — do NOT use as a headline discriminator. LiveCodeBench discriminates well (frontier ~89-92%, top open ~70%, avg ~50) AND has the best contamination resistance of any coding bench via date-windowing (--start_date/--end_date; release_v5/v6), empirically validated (DeepSeek pass@1 plunges ~60%->~0% on post-cutoff problems). BigCodeBench-Hard is the strongest pure discriminator (frontier ~30-35%, 7B models 5-16%) and not saturated. SciCode is brutally hard (frontier ~56-60%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 4.6% in 2024) — great at the frontier but a FLOOR-EFFECT RISK for small local models (an 8B likely ~0-3%), the exact failure we are avoiding. Aider polyglot shows huge spread (gpt-5 88% -> gpt-4o-mini 3.6%) but is a 2-attempt agentic edit loop (heavier). SWE-bench is agentic/non-runnable-locally and OpenAI publicly stopped reporting it (Feb 2026) over contamination — keep as cited "reported" context only.

CAUTION ON SOURCES: aggregator scrapes (llm-stats, artificialanalysis summaries, codesota) returned several CONFABULATED model names ("Claude Fable 5", "Claude Mythos", "DeepSeek-V4-Pro-Max", "Qwen3.7 Max") — I treated those names as unreliable and relied on primary repos + the patterns/percentages that recurred across sources. Percentages cited are corroborated; specific frontier model NAMES at the very top are not load-bearing here.

benchmarks.name: CRUXEval-O (output prediction)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Code REASONING: given a short Python function and an input, mentally execute and predict the OUTPUT (written as text). Sister task CRUXEval-I predicts an input for a target output.
benchmarks.license: MIT (code + dataset). Facebook Research / Meta AI.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: THE one exec-free coding option. Model writes predicted output as TEXT; gold output ships in the .jsonl dataset (code/input/output triplets); scoring = exact-match string compare. No execution of model-generated code for the -O task, so no sandbox/Docker/security exposure. Runs via any OpenAI-compatible endpoint exactly like an MCQ axis. (CRUXEval-I DOES need to execute the function on the predicted input to verify — so adopt -O only, or gate -I behind the Docker module.)
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no (for CRUXEval-O: pure string match vs gold output already in dataset). yes (for CRUXEval-I: harness executes the function on the model's predicted input). Adopt -O exec-free.
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: n/a for -O (no exec). -I would need the same Docker treatment as generation benches.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial — discriminating in 2024 (GPT-4 ~67% on -I, ~63% on -O; Code Llama 34B ~47%/44%); modern frontier has climbed into the ~80s% so headroom is shrinking but it is NOT a 1-2pt cluster like HumanEval+. Ceiling is moderate, not pinned at 100.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Good floor behaviour — small/open models score meaningfully above zero (Code Llama 34B 44-47%; smaller models lower but not 0%), because it is a 2-way-ish reasoning task with partial credit across 800 items. This is the key advantage over SciCode/LiveCodeBench for the small-local end: it discriminates WITHOUT flooring tiny models at ~0.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — fixed public set since Jan 2024, so frontier models have likely seen it; however functions were synthetically generated (Code Llama 34B) and filtered, and the task is execution-prediction not retrieval, which is harder to memorize. Mitigation fits your house style: regenerate a private variant set with your own template engine (same approach as your generated-math axis) for a contamination canary.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution_note: -O is the safe half; keep -I out of the no-sandbox lane.
benchmarks.notes: BEST FIT for a launch coding axis under your no-exec constraint. Pair: ship the public CRUXEval-O fixed subset as the scored axis + generate a private CRUXEval-style sentinel (your engine) as a contamination canary. Frame honestly on the methodology page as 'code reasoning (execution-free proxy)', NOT 'coding ability' — it does not test code authorship. Built on bigcode-evaluation-harness but you only need the trivial exact-match scorer for -O, not the harness.
used_by: Qwen3 tech report (reports CRUX-O for smaller models)
many code-LLM papers as a reasoning probe

benchmarks.name: LiveCodeBench
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Holistic, contamination-resistant competitive-programming code generation (+ self-repair, test-output prediction, code-execution sub-tasks). Problems continuously harvested from LeetCode, AtCoder, Codeforces.
benchmarks.license: MIT (repo). Problem statements are scraped contest problems — redistribution provenance is murkier than the MIT code license implies (see notes).
benchmarks.data_openness: open (dataset on HF; versioned release_v5=880 problems May2023-Jan2025, release_v6=1055 problems May2023-Apr2025).
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Generation tasks REQUIRE executing model-generated solutions against contest test cases (the harness runs the code; time limits even cause <0.5pt pass@1 variance). That is the Docker-only-opt-in trap on user machines. The test-output-prediction sub-task is closer to exec-free but is not the headline metric.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — runs generated solutions against hidden test cases (pass@1/pass@5).
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: yes but non-trivially — needs a real exec sandbox with per-test timeouts and resource limits; appropriate for a server-side or Docker-opt-in lane, not bare-metal consumer machines.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating — frontier ~89-92% (Gemini 3 Pro ~91.7%, Gemini 3 Flash ~90.8%, DeepSeek V3.2 ~89.6% on AA's view), top open ~70% (Qwen3-235B 70.7 on v5), cross-model average ~50. Still spreads the field; not pinned at ceiling. (Top model NAMES per aggregators are partly confabulated; the ~90% band and ~70% open band are corroborated by the Qwen3 tech report.)
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Discriminates but RISKS flooring the smallest models — competitive-programming difficulty means sub-7B models score low; could not verify exact 4-8B numbers (Qwen3 tech report omits LCB for <235B variants), so treat small-model floor as a flag. Mid-size code models (14-34B) clearly score >0.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low (BEST in class) — date-windowing is the whole point and is empirically validated: time-segmented eval shows DeepSeek pass@1 ~60%->~0% on post-release problems. You can pin a window strictly AFTER an anchor's training cutoff for a near-clean read.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution_note: Date-window mechanism: pass --start_date/--end_date (YYYY-MM-DD) or pick a release_version; score only problems in the window.
benchmarks.notes: Adopt ONLY inside the Docker-only opt-in coding module, NOT the no-sandbox launch lane. If adopted: pin a fixed post-cutoff window per suite version for contamination control, and budget for a real exec sandbox. Verify the licensing/redistribution status of contest problem text before you SERVE problems (MIT covers the code/harness, not necessarily LeetCode/AtCoder/Codeforces problem statements) — this is a republication risk similar to why you excluded AIME. The official leaderboard repo was last committed 2025-08-01 per your P0 notes, so frontier scores there are stale; you'd be running it yourself anyway.

benchmarks.name: HumanEval+ / MBPP+ (EvalPlus)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Function-level Python code generation correctness, with 80x (HumanEval+) / 35x (MBPP+) more tests than the originals to catch wrong-but-passing solutions. EvalPerf adds an efficiency variant.
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (code + the EvalPlus test augmentations). HumanEval base is OpenAI MIT; MBPP base is CC-BY-4.0.
benchmarks.data_openness: open. HumanEval+ = 164 problems; MBPP+ = 378 tasks (v0.2.0, down from 399). A HumanEval+-MINI exists (tests distilled ~47x).
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Scoring REQUIRES executing model-generated code against the expanded test suite. EvalPlus ships a reliability_guard (disables dangerous builtins) + multiprocessing isolation + memory cap (min 4GB) + per-test timeouts, BUT the authors themselves 'strongly recommend Docker' and put a risk warning on bare-metal runs. So: runnable, but not safely on an arbitrary user's machine without containment.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — runs generated solutions against tests; reliability_guard + subprocess limits, Docker recommended.
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: yes, relatively simply (this is the EASIEST generation bench to containerize) — small fixed Python-only problem set, no network, no repo checkout; a minimal pinned Docker image running evalplus.evaluate is realistic for a Docker-opt-in lane. HumanEval+-MINI further reduces exec cost.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: SATURATED — frontier clustered ~89-92% pass@1 (o1 89%, GPT-4o 90.2% on HumanEval; HumanEval+ leaders low-90s). Top models separated by ~1-3pts. Useless as a frontier discriminator.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Mid/large open models also score high (Llama 3.1 405B 89% HumanEval, MBPP+ 88.6%; 8B code models 70-85%), so the floor is HIGH too — small general models still get decent scores. Low discrimination across most of YOUR range; only the very smallest/non-code models fall off.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high — fixed since 2023; widely trained on. Documented memorization: top models drop 19.6-47.7 pts on transformed/perturbed variants, direct evidence of overfitting to the canonical problems.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution_note: If you ever want a containerized generation axis, this is the one to start with (smallest, simplest, well-trodden).
benchmarks.notes: Exclude as a SCORED axis: saturated at frontier + high contamination + needs exec. Its only role is (a) cited 'reported' context on model pages, or (b) the reference implementation IF you later build a minimal Docker-opt-in generation lane (its containerization is the simplest of all the exec benches). Do not put it on the headline composite.
used_by: nearly every open-model card (Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek, Granite, Phi)

benchmarks.name: BigCodeBench (Full / Hard, Complete / Instruct)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Practical code generation requiring DIVERSE library/function calls + complex multi-step instructions (not toy algorithms). 1140 tasks (Full); 148-task Hard subset with 854 unit tests. Complete=from docstring, Instruct=from NL instruction.
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (code). Tasks build on real library usage; dataset on HF.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Scoring executes generated code against unit tests that call real third-party libraries — heavier and riskier to sandbox than HumanEval+ (needs many pip deps installed in the exec env). Definitely a Docker-only-opt-in candidate, not bare-metal.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — calibrated pass@1 via greedy decoding, unit tests over real library calls.
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: yes but HARDER than EvalPlus — the exec image must provide the full set of importable libraries the tasks call; larger image, more failure surface. Server-side or heavy Docker only.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating, ESPECIALLY the Hard subset — frontier tops out ~30-35% on Hard (far from ceiling). Full set is higher but Hard is the live discriminator. One of the better non-saturated frontier signals.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Strong floor discrimination without total collapse: 7B models score ~5-16% on Hard (CodeQwen1.5-7B 15.6%, CodeGemma-7B 10.1%) — low but non-zero and rank-ordered. Good separation across the small->frontier range; arguably the best-discriminating EXEC bench end-to-end.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — newer (ICLR'25) and uses compositional library tasks that are harder to memorize than canonical HumanEval problems, but still a fixed public set. Open-data/open-weight indicators on the leaderboard help reason about per-model contamination.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution_note: Heaviest exec-env requirements of the function-level benches (many libs).
benchmarks.notes: Best-discriminating execution benchmark across your full range (Hard subset), but exec + fat dependency env make it Docker-opt-in only. If you build a generation lane later, BigCodeBench-Hard is the discriminator to use for SEPARATING models; HumanEval+ is not. Not viable for the no-sandbox launch axis.

benchmarks.name: Aider polyglot benchmark
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Real-world code EDITING across 6 languages (C++, Go, Java, JavaScript, Python, Rust): solve 225 of Exercism's hardest exercises, 2 attempts (second attempt sees failing unit-test output). Tests both coding and diff/edit-format reliability.
benchmarks.license: Benchmark harness is part of Aider (Apache-2.0). Underlying problems are Exercism exercises — Exercism content is generally open-source-licensed but per-track/per-exercise licensing varies and must be checked before redistribution.
benchmarks.data_openness: open-ish (Exercism), but redistribution license must be verified per the above.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Runs unit tests against model-edited files (executes generated code) AND drives a 2-attempt agentic edit loop with a specific edit-format (whole-file / diff). More moving parts than a single-shot generation bench; needs both exec sandbox and the Aider scaffolding. Not a clean OpenAI-endpoint-only single-call axis.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — pass rate = all unit tests pass after attempt 2.
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: yes but it is an agent loop, so containment is heavier (per-language toolchains: g++, go, java, node, cargo, python). Multi-language exec env = large image; Docker-opt-in only.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating with HUGE spread — verified on the official leaderboard (last updated 2025-11-20): gpt-5 (high) 88.0%, gpt-5 (medium) 86.7%, o3-pro 84.9%, gemini-2.5-pro 83.1% at the top, down to gpt-4o-mini 3.6%. ~84-point range. Excellent separator.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floors weak models hard (gpt-4o-mini 3.6%), so the smallest local models will likely cluster near 0 here — discriminates at the top/mid but risks a floor effect for tiny local models, similar concern to SciCode though less extreme.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — Exercism problems are public and old; mitigated somewhat by multi-language + edit-format difficulty, but no date-windowing. Less contamination-resistant than LiveCodeBench.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution_note: Multi-language toolchain exec = the fattest sandbox of any bench here.
benchmarks.notes: Great real-world signal and spread, but it is an agentic multi-language edit loop = the heaviest to run/sandbox and the furthest from a simple OpenAI-endpoint axis. Keep as cited context and/or a future server-side lane; not a community-bare-metal axis. Verify Exercism per-exercise licensing before serving problems.
used_by: widely cited as the SWE-bench-Python-monoculture alternative

benchmarks.name: SciCode
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Research-grade scientific coding: 80 main problems decomposed into ~338 subproblems (288 in test set) across 16 science disciplines; integrates domain knowledge + programming. Gold solutions + scientist-written test cases.
benchmarks.license: Could not verify a specific license from primary sources within this pass (repo is scicode-bench/SciCode; treat license as UNVERIFIED — flag). Apache/MIT plausible but unconfirmed.
benchmarks.data_openness: open (problems + gold + tests on the project site/GitHub), license TBD.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Scoring executes generated code against scientist-written test cases (needs scientific Python stack: numpy/scipy/etc). Exec-based + heavy scientific deps = Docker-opt-in only.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — runs generated code against ~4 test cases per subproblem.
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: yes (scientific-Python image), similar weight to BigCodeBench.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating AND far from saturated at the frontier — top models only ~56-60% (per AA: ~60.2% / 58.9% / 56.6% for the three leaders; exact NAMES from AA are confabulated, percentages are the signal). Excellent frontier headroom.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: FLOOR-EFFECT RISK — extremely hard: best model in the 2024 paper (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) solved only 4.6% in the realistic setting. Small local models will almost certainly score ~0-3%, i.e. it does NOT discriminate at the small-local end (the exact failure mode local-bench is trying to avoid). Great for ranking frontier anchors, poor for ranking a 7-14B field.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low-med — niche research problems are harder to have memorized; but fixed public set, no date-windowing.
benchmarks.notes: Exclude for local-bench's purpose: it floors small local models near 0, so it cannot separate YOUR core audience even though it is a beautiful frontier discriminator. Plus exec + unverified license. Could appear only as cited 'reported' frontier context. (Mirror-image of HumanEval+: HumanEval+ is saturated at the TOP, SciCode is saturated at the BOTTOM for your range.)

benchmarks.name: RepoBench
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Repository-level code auto-COMPLETION (retrieval + next-line/snippet prediction) using cross-file context, Python & Java. Three settings (retrieval, completion, pipeline).
benchmarks.license: Data derived from github-code (open-source-licensed GitHub repos); benchmark code on GitHub. License is permissive but verify exact terms before redistributing code snippets.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Completion is scored by similarity to the held-out next line(s) — exact-match / edit-similarity (e.g. EM, edit-sim, CodeBLEU) rather than necessarily executing model code, so a completion-only lane CAN be largely exec-free. BUT it is a completion/FIM-style task, not an instruct/chat task — awkward over a chat OpenAI endpoint, and string-similarity scoring is fragile/answer-extraction-sensitive.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no (completion scored by string/edit similarity to gold next-line) — though it is similarity-based, not pass/fail correctness.
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: n/a (no exec for the completion metric).
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial/unclear at current frontier — older (2023) benchmark; not a headline frontier discriminator in 2025-2026 and rarely reported on new model cards. Likely partially saturated / superseded by SWE-bench-family and LiveCodeBench for repo-level claims.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Completion similarity gives partial credit, so small models score non-zero, but the metric (edit similarity) is noisy and not a clean correctness signal.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med — explicitly mitigated by date-windowing the test set (repos created Feb-Aug 2023, after the Mar-2022 training-data cutoff of the base set), which is a genuine contamination control. But the window is now old, so modern models may have trained past it.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution_note: One of the few exec-free options, but FIM-completion shape + similarity scoring make it a poor fit for a chat-endpoint, correctness-based suite.
benchmarks.notes: Exclude: although it is exec-free (a plus), it is a FIM/completion task scored by string similarity, which clashes with your chat-endpoint + crisp-correctness design and is answer-extraction-fragile. CRUXEval-O is the better exec-free coding axis (clean exact-match, chat-shaped, partial-credit across 800 items). Keep RepoBench only as a reference for the date-windowing idea.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench / SWE-bench Verified (cross-ref: agentic axis)
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Resolve REAL GitHub issues end-to-end: given a repo + issue, produce a patch that makes the hidden test suite pass. Verified = 500 human-validated solvable tasks. The canonical agentic coding benchmark.
benchmarks.license: MIT (harness). Task data = real OSS repos (their own licenses); patches/issues public.
benchmarks.data_openness: open, but operationally gated behind heavy infra.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Requires per-task repo checkout, building the project, an agent scaffold (file nav + edit + run tests), and executing the project's full test suite in isolation — exactly the sandbox/agent/external-dependency trap the brief calls out. Not runnable on a consumer machine via an OpenAI endpoint; vendor scores even depend heavily on the agent harness (10-30pt swings from scaffolding alone).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: yes — builds repos and runs test suites against generated patches.
benchmarks.can_be_sandboxed: yes only with substantial server-side infra (per-repo Docker images, the SWE-bench harness). Out of scope for community bare-metal entirely.
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating-ish but COMPROMISED — frontier ~80% on Verified (vendor-tuned harnesses higher); BUT OpenAI published 'Why SWE-bench Verified no longer measures frontier coding progress' (Feb 2026) and STOPPED reporting it, citing models reproducing gold patches / problem specifics from training data. Trust is eroding; SWE-bench Pro is the proposed successor.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Floors small local models near 0 (they cannot drive the full agent loop) — irrelevant for your small-local audience.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high — documented gold-patch/problem-statement leakage into training data; the reason OpenAI deprecated it.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution_note: Full repo build + test-suite exec per task.
benchmarks.notes: Keep ONLY as cited 'reported' context on model pages (it is the famous agentic-coding number users expect to see), exactly as your v0 plan already lists it among non-runnable reported benches. Never an anchor-run or community axis. Its deprecation by OpenAI is a useful methodology talking point about why local-bench runs its own contamination-resistant suite.

top_picks_for_local_suite: CRUXEval-O (output prediction) — THE launch coding axis: the ONLY exec-free coding benchmark (model writes predicted output as text; exact-match vs gold output already in the MIT-licensed dataset; no sandbox/Docker/untrusted-code on user machines). Discriminates without flooring small models; ship a fixed public subset + generate a private CRUXEval-style sentinel (your template engine) as a contamination canary. Frame as 'code reasoning (execution-free proxy)', not 'coding ability'.

DEFER all execution-based coding to a clearly-labelled Docker-only OPT-IN module (matches your existing plan). Authors agree exec on bare metal is unsafe (EvalPlus 'strongly recommends Docker' + ships a risk warning).

If/when you build that Docker lane: use BigCodeBench-Hard as the DISCRIMINATOR (frontier ~30-35%, 7B ~5-16%, not saturated, separates the full range) and LiveCodeBench with a pinned post-cutoff date-window as the CONTAMINATION-RESISTANT generation axis (empirically catches leakage: DeepSeek ~60%->~0% post-cutoff). HumanEval+/MBPP+ only as the simplest reference impl to containerize first — NOT as a scored headline (saturated ~89-92% + high contamination).

EXCLUDE as scored axes: HumanEval+/MBPP+ (saturated at frontier), SciCode (floors small local models ~0-3% — kills small-model discrimination, the exact failure you're fixing), RepoBench (exec-free but FIM/similarity-scored, fragile, chat-mismatched), SWE-bench/Aider (agentic, heavy multi-toolchain sandbox, not bare-metal-runnable).

KEEP as cited 'reported' context only on model pages (users expect them): SWE-bench Verified, Aider polyglot, LiveCodeBench frontier numbers — labelled 'reported', never charted on your measured axes.

LICENSE WATCH before SERVING any problems: LiveCodeBench (LeetCode/AtCoder/Codeforces problem-statement redistribution is murkier than its MIT code license — AIME-style risk), Aider (per-exercise Exercism licensing varies), SciCode (license UNVERIFIED — flag). CRUXEval (MIT) and EvalPlus (Apache-2.0) are clean.
