axis_summary: Across all three axes the pattern is the same: the v0-era table-stakes benchmarks (IFEval, MGSM, NIAH, plain MMLU-translations) are SATURATED at the frontier and add little discrimination, while the genuinely discriminating successors mostly carry one of two costs that bite our specific product (local users on a single consumer GPU via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint): they need an LLM-as-judge (MultiChallenge, InfoBench, FollowBench, HELMET's QA/summarization subtasks) or they need a large KV/context budget the local user may not have (RULER/HELMET/LongBench-v2 at 64k-128k+). Recommended core picks that are clean on BOTH licensing AND local-runnability: IFBench (instruction-following; Apache-2.0 code / ODC-BY data, fully programmatic verifier functions, top model ~83% so still discriminating, out-of-domain constraints = contamination-resistant) and a curated RULER subset (long-context; Apache-2.0, fully synthetic so contamination-resistant and regenerable, sharp small-model collapse 32k->128k gives bottom-of-range discrimination) capped at a context tier the user opts into (e.g. 16k/32k/64k). For multilingual the best discriminating-and-clean option is INCLUDE (Apache-2.0, 44 languages, native regional-exam MCQ so far less contaminated than translated MMLU, programmatic) or Global-MMLU (Apache-2.0, 42 langs, MCQ, has culturally-sensitive/agnostic split); both discriminate hard once low-resource languages are included (small models ~18-40% vs >80% English). IFEval stays as a saturated-but-cheap table-stakes baseline (programmatic, Apache-2.0, and unlike the others it has a usable FLOOR — 7-8B models sit ~72-74%, so it still separates weak local models from decent ones even though it compresses at the top). KEY LANDMINES: (1) MultiChallenge/InfoBench/FollowBench require a strong external judge model — adopting any of them forces us to either ship a server-side judge (breaks the "runs entirely on your endpoint" promise and adds our cost/latency) or accept judge noise; treat as server-side-scored optional modules, never CLI-local. (2) Multi-IF data is CC-BY-NC-2.0 (non-commercial) — its IFEval-style scoring is otherwise ideal and it doubles as multilingual+IF, but the non-commercial data license conflicts with any future monetization and with us serving the items, so EXCLUDE unless we get a commercial grant or rebuild equivalent items ourselves. (3) Long-context benchmarks are the single biggest local-runnability trap: the discrimination only appears at 32k-128k+, which many consumer single-GPU setups cannot reach with usable KV cache (mirrors our own P0 finding that 27B-FP8 KV didn't fit a 32GB card) — so long-context must be a separate opt-in tier with explicit context-budget gating, reporting "effective context length" (RULER-style threshold) rather than one scalar, and is the right home for the deferred long-context module rather than the launch core. (4) Saturation is bimodal: distinguish "saturated at frontier only" (IFEval, MGSM, MMMLU/Global-MMLU in English+major langs — still discriminate small local models) from "saturated even for small models / floor effect" (NIAH passes ~98% for nearly everyone; HLE-style would floor at 0) — the former are still useful at the LOCAL end which is our wedge, the latter are dead weight. Net recommendation for the suite: add IFBench (IF), add a RULER subset behind a context-tier gate (long-context module), add INCLUDE or Global-MMLU (multilingual) — keep IFEval as a labeled table-stakes baseline; hold MultiChallenge/FollowBench/HELMET as server-side-judged optional modules; exclude Multi-IF (NC license), NIAH and MGSM as standalone scored axes (saturated; OK only as illustrative/diagnostic), and ZeroSCROLLS (superseded, short contexts).

benchmarks.name: IFEval (google/IFEval)
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (dataset card + official checker code in google-research repo, both Apache-2.0)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 541 prompts, fully programmatic constraint checkers (no judge, no code-exec, no external API). Already vendored in our v0 suite. Short prompts, trivial context. Runs against any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Single-turn verifiable instruction following: ~25 constraint types (format, length, keyword inclusion/exclusion, case, JSON, etc.) auto-checked.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - frontier models score >80-90% (prompt/instruction strict). Our own v0 run had it narrow at 0.83-0.98. Compresses the top of the range badly.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: NOT floored - 7-8B instruct models score ~72-74% (Qwen2.5-7B 72.6% prompt-strict, Llama-3.1-8B ~73.9%). So it still separates weak/broken local models from competent ones; usable floor for the local wedge even though the ceiling is jammed.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med - public since Nov 2023, widely trained-on; constraints are templated so the specific prompts can be memorized. No canary/rotation.
benchmarks.notes: Keep as a LABELED table-stakes baseline (cheap, clean, already integrated) but do NOT rely on it for frontier-vs-frontier discrimination. Pair with IFBench which was built specifically because IFEval saturated and overfits. Task #13 (checker parity vs official impl) is the right hardening step.
used_by: most lab model cards historically (though increasingly dropped)

benchmarks.name: IFBench (allenai/IFBench)
benchmarks.license: Code Apache-2.0; dataset ODC-BY-1.0 (Open Data Commons Attribution; research/educational use per Ai2 Responsible Use Guidelines)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 58 out-of-domain verifiable constraints attached to held-out WildChat prompts + 29 IFTrain constraints, each with a programmatic verification function. No LLM judge, no code-exec sandbox, no external API. Open on HuggingFace ungated. Short prompts. Ideal for CLI-local scoring against an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: GENERALIZATION of instruction following to NEW/unseen verifiable constraints (vs IFEval's reused constraint set) - precise constraint following on out-of-domain instructions; also a multi-turn variant.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - explicitly built because IFEval saturated. At launch (Jul 2025) top models (Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude 4 Sonnet) capped ~50%. As of 2026 the AA leaderboard top is Grok 4.3 ~83.3% - still well below ceiling and spreading models, so it remains a discriminating frontier axis.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Not floored but hard - small models score meaningfully lower than the ~50-83% frontier band (paper shows large headroom across the post-training spectrum). Good top-to-bottom spread; the OOD design means small models can't lean on memorized IFEval constraints.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low-med - constraints are deliberately out-of-domain/novel vs the training-saturated IFEval set; prompts are held-out WildChat. Lower memorization risk than IFEval. Still public so will erode over time; an IFTrain/test split helps.
benchmarks.notes: TOP NEW PICK for the instruction-following axis. Drop-in for our existing programmatic IF lane, replaces/augments IFEval for frontier discrimination, license is servable (Apache code + ODC-BY data with attribution). Verify our vendored verifier functions match the repo (same parity discipline as IFEval task #13). Use the single-turn set for the core axis; multi-turn set is an optional extension.

benchmarks.name: MultiChallenge (ScaleAI/MultiChallenge)
benchmarks.license: Open-sourced (data + code on GitHub ekwinox117/multi-challenge and HF ScaleAI/MultiChallenge); specific OSS license string not confirmed on the dataset card - VERIFY exact license before serving.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Scoring uses LLM-AS-JUDGE with instance-level binary rubrics (the authors report 93% human alignment with their rubric method vs 36% naive). A local user cannot score this on their own endpoint without ALSO running a strong judge model - that breaks the 'runs entirely on your local endpoint' promise and pushes judge cost/latency/noise onto us if server-side. Multi-turn conversations (longer prompts but not extreme context).
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Realistic MULTI-TURN conversation: instruction retention across turns, inference-memory of user info, reliable versioned editing, self-coherence.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - strongly. Designed so items must break >=3 of 6 reference models. 2026 leaderboard spreads top models widely: Gemini 3.1 Pro ~71.4%, GPT-5.4 Pro ~69.2%, o3-Pro ~62.4%, Claude Opus 4.5 (thinking) ~59.0%, Gemini 2.5 Pro ~53.6%. One of the better frontier discriminators on this axis.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Not floored but very hard - original paper had ALL frontier models <50% (best Claude 3.5 Sonnet 41.4%); small local models will score low, so risk of bunching near the bottom (weak discrimination among the smallest models, strong among capable ones).
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med - public test set, synthetic-then-human-edited; multi-turn rubric format is harder to memorize than static QA but the prompts are fixed and public.
benchmarks.notes: Best-in-class for MULTI-TURN IF discrimination, but the LLM-judge dependency makes it a SERVER-SIDE-SCORED optional module, never a CLI-local axis. If adopted, we run the judge (cost + must publish judge model/version for reproducibility, and judge drift becomes a comparability risk across suite versions). Good candidate for a later 'conversation' module rather than launch core.
used_by: cited widely as the multi-turn standard

benchmarks.name: Multi-IF (facebook/Multi-IF)
benchmarks.license: Code Apache-2.0; DATASET CC-BY-NC-2.0 (NON-COMMERCIAL). Repo archived/read-only since Dec 2025.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Scoring is programmatic IFEval-style (ifeval.py) - no judge, runnable locally on an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. BUT the data is non-commercial, so we cannot serve it as part of a product that monetizes or carries aggregate-data rights, and redistribution via our API is constrained.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: MULTILINGUAL (8 languages) MULTI-TURN (3-turn) verifiable instruction following - doubles as both an IF and a multilingual axis; measures cross-turn instruction-retention degradation (e.g. o1-preview 87.7% turn1 -> 70.7% turn3).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - turn-over-turn degradation and non-Latin-script drops (Hindi/Russian/Chinese < English) spread models; best models ~78-79% three-turn average, so headroom remains.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Not floored - multilingual + multi-turn compounding makes it hard for small models; good spread, but smallest models may bunch low on non-Latin scripts.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med - public since Oct 2024; templated IFEval-style constraints are memorizable; no rotation.
benchmarks.notes: EXCLUDE for the product despite excellent design: the CC-BY-NC-2.0 DATA license conflicts with our monetization/aggregate-rights stance and with serving items via our API (same class of problem as GPQA's non-republication, here it's the license not author intent). Mitigation if we want this capability: build our own multilingual multi-turn IFEval-style items (we already own a generated-math engine; an analogous generated multilingual-IF generator would be license-clean AND contamination-resistant). Use Multi-IF only as an internal reference/sanity check, not a served axis.
used_by: cited as the multilingual multi-turn IF standard

benchmarks.name: InfoBench (DRFR)
benchmarks.license: Data + code public (MIT on the repo, commonly); VERIFY exact license string before serving.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: DRFR (Decomposed Requirements Following Ratio) is scored by an LLM JUDGE - GPT-4 (1106-preview) answers the decomposed boolean sub-questions. Not CLI-local without a strong judge; same server-side-judge problem as MultiChallenge.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Instruction following decomposed into atomic requirements (content/linguistic/style/format/number); 500 instructions -> 2,250 decomposed boolean checks. Finer-grained than IFEval's pass/fail.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial - more saturated than IFBench/FollowBench; strong models score high DRFR. The decomposition gives partial credit, which inflates scores and reduces top-end spread relative to FollowBench's all-or-nothing HSR.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Not floored - DRFR partial credit means small models get non-zero, decent spread in the mid-range.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med - public since early 2024; fixed prompts.
benchmarks.notes: EXCLUDE from the local/CLI core: the GPT-4 judge dependency plus only partial frontier discrimination make it weak value for our constraints. The DRFR decomposition IDEA is worth borrowing for our own programmatic IF reporting (per-constraint pass rates), but we can get that from IFBench/IFEval verifier functions without any judge.

benchmarks.name: FollowBench (YJiangcm/FollowBench)
benchmarks.license: Data + code public on GitHub; exact license string NOT confirmed in repo metadata - VERIFY before serving.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Evaluation requires a GPT-4 API key (LLM judge) for the open-ended constraint categories; Hard Satisfaction Rate is computed from judge verdicts. Not CLI-local without a strong judge.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: MULTI-LEVEL fine-grained constraint following across 5 types (content, situation, style, format, example) with increasing constraint count per level; Hard Satisfaction Rate (HSR) = ALL constraints met simultaneously (strict, no partial credit).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - the all-or-nothing HSR and escalating constraint levels keep it harder/less saturated than InfoBench at the top; constraint-count scaling exposes where models break down.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Not floored but strict HSR is punishing - small models can collapse to low HSR at higher levels (some bunching at the bottom on the hardest levels).
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med - public since 2023 (ACL 2024); fixed prompts.
benchmarks.notes: Better frontier discrimination than InfoBench because of strict HSR + level scaling, BUT same GPT-4-judge local-runnability strike. Treat as an optional SERVER-SIDE-JUDGED module if/when we add judged IF; otherwise IFBench (programmatic, no judge) covers the same need cleanly. The 'increasing-constraint-level' design is a good model for difficulty-stratified reporting in our own programmatic items.

benchmarks.name: RULER (NVIDIA/RULER)
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (code + synthetic task generators)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Fully SYNTHETIC - tasks are generated, no fixed dataset to gate; scoring is programmatic (substring/exact match on retrieved keys/values), NO judge. Runs against any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. THE CATCH: discrimination lives at 32k-128k+ context; a single consumer GPU often can't allocate that KV cache (our own P0: 27B-FP8 KV didn't fit a 32GB card). So local-runnable ONLY up to whatever context the user's rig supports -> must gate by a context TIER the user opts into.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Real effective context length via 13 synthetic tasks: 8 needle-in-haystack retrieval variants, 2 aggregation, 2 QA, 1 multi-hop variable tracing - tested at 4k/8k/16k/32k/64k/128k.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial/discriminating by length - the simple NIAH subtasks are saturated (frontier ~near-perfect at moderate lengths), but aggregation/multi-hop/QA at 64k-128k still separate models and the 'effective context length' (RULER threshold ~85.6%, Llama-2-7B@4k baseline) cleanly ranks who actually holds their advertised window.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: STRONG bottom-range discrimination - small models collapse sharply with length: Llama-3.1-8B drops >10 points from 64k->128k; many 7-8B models fall below threshold well under their claimed window. This is exactly the small-local-vs-frontier spread we want - IF the user can run the length.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low - synthetic and regenerable per run with fresh random needles/keys (canary by construction); we can generate private variants like our generated-math axis. Best contamination profile of any long-context option.
benchmarks.notes: TOP long-context pick, but as a SEPARATE OPT-IN CONTEXT-TIER MODULE, not launch core. Report effective-context-length (threshold-crossing) rather than one scalar; offer fixed tiers (e.g. 8k/16k/32k/64k) so users run what their KV budget allows and we compare within a tier (lanes that never merge, like our reasoning lanes). Drop or down-weight the saturated NIAH subtasks; keep aggregation/multi-hop/QA. Synthetic generators are license-clean and let us mint a private sentinel set.
used_by: most 2025 long-context model reports (Qwen2.5-1M, Jamba-1.5, Llama-3.1, xGen)

benchmarks.name: HELMET (princeton-nlp/HELMET)
benchmarks.license: MIT (code). Underlying task datasets carry their own upstream licenses (mixed) - must audit per-subtask before serving.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Mixed: Recall/RAG/passage-reranking/citation subtasks are programmatic (substring); but LongQA and Summarization need a GPT-4o JUDGE (model-based eval). Supports API endpoints (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Together) so no mandatory local GPU for the model under test, but the judged subtasks need our judge, and contexts run to 128k (same KV-budget ceiling as RULER for local rigs).
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Application-centric long-context across 7 categories: retrieval-augmented generation, passage re-ranking, generation-with-citations, long-doc QA, summarization, many-shot ICL, synthetic recall - controllable lengths to 128k.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - designed to fix NIAH/ZeroSCROLLS saturation; spreads 50+ models including reasoning models across realistic tasks and lengths. Less saturated than NIAH; good frontier separation on the harder subtasks.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Not floored - graded tasks + few-shot prompting give signal even for base/small models; long contexts still punish small models hard.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med - built partly from existing datasets (some pre-2024, memorizable); not synthetic like RULER, so higher contamination exposure on the non-synthetic subtasks.
benchmarks.notes: Strong, realistic long-context battery, but heavier than RULER for us: per-subtask license audit needed + GPT-4o judge for 2 of 7 categories + 128k contexts. Best fit as the programmatic-subset (RAG/recall/reranking/citation) of an advanced long-context module AFTER RULER; reserve the judged subtasks for server-side scoring. Not launch-core given the local-runnability and licensing friction.

benchmarks.name: LongBench v2 (zai-org/THUDM LongBench-v2)
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (dataset card + repo)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 503 four-option MCQ -> programmatic exact-match on the answer letter, NO judge (big advantage over HELMET/MultiChallenge). Open/ungated on HF. THE CATCH: contexts span 8k to 2M WORDS (majority under 128k, but a real tail well beyond consumer KV budgets) - so local-runnable only for the subset that fits the user's context window; needs length-tier gating like RULER.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Deep long-context UNDERSTANDING + REASONING (not just retrieval) across 6 categories: single-doc QA, multi-doc QA, long in-context learning, long-dialogue history, code-repo understanding, long structured-data understanding.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - hard: best DIRECT-answer model ~50.1%, best with extended reasoning (o1-preview) ~57.7%; human experts 53.7% under 15min. Far from saturated; reasoning helps, which makes it a good reasoning-x-long-context axis.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Risk of FLOOR effect at the bottom - 4-option MCQ floors at 25% random; a struggling small model at extreme length may sit near chance, compressing discrimination among the weakest models. Mid-to-frontier spread is good.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med - public since Dec 2024; fixed MCQ set, memorizable answer keys (MCQ letter leakage is the classic risk). MCQ format also lets a model sometimes answer from priors without reading the context (mitigate by checking no-context baseline).
benchmarks.notes: The best NO-JUDGE, license-clean long-context reasoning option - attractive because programmatic MCQ scoring fits our CLI-local model. Use as the reasoning-flavored complement to RULER inside the long-context module, restricted to a context tier (e.g. items <=32k or <=64k) so consumer rigs can run it; report a no-context control to catch prior-leakage. Not launch-core (long-context is deferred) but the top pick if/when we add a realistic (non-synthetic) long-context task.
used_by: LongBench is a widely cited family

benchmarks.name: ZeroSCROLLS
benchmarks.license: MIT (code); component datasets carry upstream licenses (mixed).
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Zero-shot long-doc tasks; some metrics programmatic, but contexts are SHORT by 2026 standards (built for ~early long-context era) and several tasks/leaderboard practices lean on generation metrics. Runnable but low value.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Zero-shot long-text understanding (summarization, QA, aggregation over documents) - the 2023-era predecessor to RULER/HELMET.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - superseded; its underlying tasks (and contexts) are too short/easy for current frontier LCLMs (e.g. RULER notes SQuAD-v2-derived tasks are effectively saturated). Frontier models cluster near the top.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: n/a - too easy/short to give meaningful small-vs-frontier spread at modern context lengths.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high - older (2023), built from pre-existing public datasets heavily represented in training data.
benchmarks.notes: EXCLUDE - explicitly superseded by RULER and HELMET (both cite its insufficient lengths and task coverage). No reason to adopt over RULER (synthetic, contamination-clean) or LongBench v2 (harder, no-judge MCQ).

benchmarks.name: NIAH (Needle-in-a-Haystack, generic)
benchmarks.license: N/A - a synthetic TASK TEMPLATE (gkamradt original is MIT); no fixed dataset.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: Fully synthetic, programmatic substring check, no judge, generate-on-the-fly. Trivial to run. Length-limited only by the user's context budget.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Single-fact retrieval from a long context (insert a 'needle' sentence, ask for it).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - frontier models score ~98%+; explicitly called out as no longer a robust long-context indicator.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Largely saturated even for many small models at moderate lengths (the easy variant) -> floor/ceiling effect; only at extreme lengths or multi-needle does it separate.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low - synthetic/regenerable, but irrelevant given saturation.
benchmarks.notes: EXCLUDE as a scored axis - saturated table-stakes. Keep ONLY as a free diagnostic/sanity check (it's already a subset of RULER) or as a smoke test that an endpoint reads its context at all. RULER's harder subtasks subsume it.
used_by: subsumed into RULER and most long-context suites

benchmarks.name: MGSM (Multilingual Grade School Math)
benchmarks.license: Public (GSM8K is MIT; MGSM translations released openly, used in openai/simple-evals and lm-eval-harness). Confirm the translation-set license string before serving.
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 250 GSM8K problems x 10 languages; programmatic numeric-answer extraction/match (simple-evals provides the scorer), NO judge, short prompts. Runs against any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multilingual mathematical reasoning (chain-of-thought) across 10 typologically diverse languages incl. low-resource (Swahili, Bengali, Telugu, Thai).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: saturated - frontier reasoning models exceed 95% (Claude Opus 4.5 thinking ~95.2%, GPT-5.x ~96%); top-10 all >90%. No frontier discrimination left; successors like MGSM-Pro exist.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Partly discriminating at the bottom - small models still vary, and low-resource languages (Swahili/Bengali/Telugu) drop sharply for weak models, so it separates small-local models even though the top is jammed. But it conflates math ability with multilingual ability (a weak-math model fails regardless of language).
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high - GSM8K is one of the most contaminated datasets in existence; translations are public since 2022. Heavy memorization risk.
benchmarks.notes: EXCLUDE as a standalone scored axis (saturated + heavily contaminated + confounds math with multilingual). If we want a multilingual-reasoning signal, our OWN generated-math engine could emit translated/templated variants (license-clean + contamination-resistant) - far better than MGSM. Keep MGSM only as an external cross-reference number.

benchmarks.name: MMMLU (openai/MMMLU)
benchmarks.license: MIT (dataset card)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: MMLU test set professionally human-translated into 14 languages; 4-option MCQ -> programmatic exact-match, NO judge. Open/ungated, MIT. Same trivial local-runnability as our existing MMLU-Pro lane. Short prompts.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multilingual knowledge + reasoning (MMLU's 57 subjects) across 14 languages incl. low-resource (Yoruba, Swahili, Bengali).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial - inherits MMLU saturation in English + major languages (frontier ~88%+, Vellum/AA call base MMLU 'outdated'); BUT low-resource languages remain unsaturated even at frontier, so the low-resource slices still discriminate top models.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: STRONG discrimination on low-resource slices - small models collapse: reported low-resource avg ~18.1% vs high-resource ~36.1%; the high-resource/low-resource GAP is itself a clean discriminator. English slice is saturated/floored-high for capable models.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: high - it IS MMLU (the most contaminated knowledge benchmark) translated; English answers are memorized, and translation can leak via parallel data. Low-resource slices are somewhat less contaminated.
benchmarks.notes: Cleanest LICENSE (MIT) and cleanest local-runnability (MCQ, no judge) of the multilingual options, and we already run an MMLU-family MCQ scorer. BUT high contamination + English-side saturation. If used, score ONLY (or weight toward) the low-resource language slices where it still discriminates, and report the high-vs-low-resource gap. Global-MMLU/INCLUDE are better-curated; MMMLU is the pragmatic minimal-effort add given our existing MCQ pipeline.

benchmarks.name: Global-MMLU (CohereForAI/Global-MMLU)
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (dataset card)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 42 languages, 4-option MCQ -> programmatic exact-match, NO judge. Open/ungated, Apache-2.0. Has a 'Lite' version (Global-MMLU-Lite) for a practical fixed item count. Same MCQ pipeline we already have. Short prompts.
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multilingual knowledge/reasoning (MMLU base) across 42 languages, IMPROVED translation quality + professional/community verification, WITH a culturally-sensitive vs culturally-agnostic question split (lets us separate language ability from Western-knowledge bias).
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: partial - English + major-language slices inherit MMLU saturation; low-resource slices and the culturally-sensitive subset still discriminate (rankings CHANGE on the culturally-sensitive subset, which is a useful frontier-discriminating signal the raw translations lack).
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Strong low-resource discrimination - small models drop to <40% on many low-resource languages vs >80% English; wide spread. The culturally-agnostic subset isolates pure language ability.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: med-high - MMLU-derived (contaminated base), but the re-translation + cultural annotation reduce naive leakage vs MMMLU; still public and memorizable on the agnostic subset.
benchmarks.notes: TOP multilingual pick alongside INCLUDE. Apache-2.0 (cleanest license), no judge, MCQ (fits our pipeline), and the culturally-sensitive/agnostic split + Lite size make it the most product-ready multilingual option. Use Global-MMLU-Lite as the fixed stratified item set; report a low-resource slice and the cultural-sensitivity split. Pair with INCLUDE if we want a less-contaminated native-exam complement.

benchmarks.name: INCLUDE (CohereForAI/include-base-44)
benchmarks.license: Apache-2.0 (dataset card)
benchmarks.local_runnable_reason: 22,637 four-option MCQ across 44 languages; programmatic exact-match, NO judge. Open/ungated, Apache-2.0. Fits our existing MCQ pipeline. Items are exam questions (moderate prompt length, not long-context).
benchmarks.what_it_measures: Multilingual language understanding with REGIONAL/CULTURAL knowledge - compiled from NATIVE academic/professional/licensing exams in 44 languages (15 scripts, 58 domains), NOT translated from English. Measures real in-language deployment ability, not English-knowledge-via-translation.
benchmarks.requires_code_execution: no
benchmarks.saturation_at_frontier: discriminating - models do well in major languages but struggle with culturally-specific/less-represented languages and topics; native regional content keeps frontier headroom that translated MMLU lacks.
benchmarks.saturation_at_small_models: Strong discrimination - small models struggle broadly on native non-English exam content; wide small-to-frontier spread, especially on low-resource languages and region-specific domains.
benchmarks.contamination_risk: low-med - NATIVE exam content (not English-MMLU translated) means far less overlap with the heavily-contaminated English benchmark corpus; some public exams may still appear in training data, but the contamination profile is the BEST of the multilingual options.
benchmarks.notes: BEST multilingual pick on CONTAMINATION + genuine in-language measurement; Apache-2.0, no judge, MCQ. The native-exam design directly addresses the Western-centric-leakage problem that makes MMMLU/Global-MMLU partly saturated. Use a fixed stratified subset (it's large - 22.6k items) covering a language tier; report low-resource slices. Pair with Global-MMLU (which adds the cultural-sensitivity split + Lite convenience). Together INCLUDE + Global-MMLU give a contamination-resistant, license-clean, judge-free multilingual axis.

top_picks_for_local_suite: IFBench - instruction-following CORE: Apache-2.0 code / ODC-BY data, fully programmatic verifiers (no judge), open on HF, still discriminating (top ~83% in 2026), out-of-domain constraints = contamination-resistant. Drop-in replacement/augment for our saturated IFEval lane.

INCLUDE - multilingual CORE: Apache-2.0, judge-free MCQ, native regional-exam content (best contamination profile of any multilingual bench), strong small-to-frontier spread on low-resource languages. Use a fixed stratified language-tier subset.

Global-MMLU (Lite) - multilingual SECONDARY/complement: Apache-2.0, judge-free MCQ, culturally-sensitive/agnostic split that separates language ability from Western-knowledge bias; Lite version gives a practical fixed item set. Pairs with INCLUDE.

IFEval - instruction-following TABLE-STAKES baseline (keep, labeled): Apache-2.0, programmatic, already integrated, has a usable FLOOR (7-8B ~72-74%) so it still separates weak local models even though it is saturated at the frontier. Do NOT use for frontier discrimination.

RULER subset - LONG-CONTEXT module (deferred, opt-in context-tier): Apache-2.0, fully synthetic (contamination-clean + regenerable as a private sentinel), sharp small-model collapse 32k->128k. Gate by a context tier the user can actually run; report effective-context-length, not one scalar; drop the saturated NIAH subtasks. This is the right home for the deferred long-context module.

DEFER/SERVER-SIDE-ONLY: MultiChallenge + FollowBench (best multi-turn / strict-constraint discriminators but require an LLM judge -> server-side-scored optional modules, never CLI-local) and LongBench v2 / HELMET programmatic subset (no-judge but need 32k-128k+ context).

EXCLUDE: Multi-IF (CC-BY-NC data conflicts with monetization/serving), MGSM (saturated >95% + heavily contaminated + confounds math with language), NIAH (saturated ~98%, keep only as a smoke test), ZeroSCROLLS (superseded by RULER/HELMET), InfoBench (GPT-4-judge + only partially discriminating). For multilingual MATH and multilingual MULTI-TURN IF, prefer extending our OWN generated-math/IF engine to translated/templated variants - license-clean AND contamination-resistant - over adopting MGSM/Multi-IF.

summary: This is the composite-validity meta track for local-bench's suite rebuild. The existing scoring methodology (docs/scoring-methodology.md, v2, already GPT-5.5 red-teamed) is sound and aligns strongly with current practice; this research VALIDATES it against 2025-26 primary sources and sharpens four open calls. Core finding: there is NO single "saturation-proof normalization" — saturation is a BENCH-QUALITY problem (item selection so models actually MISS), and normalization choice only determines how honestly you express what's left. The field has split into two camps: (A) ABSOLUTE chance-corrected / headroom-buffered means (Artificial Analysis: pass@1 averaged, Elo components clamped via clamp((Elo-500)/2000); LLM-Stats uses a headroom buffer mapping SOTA→80 leaving 20% ceiling room) — temporally STABLE, the right choice for "track MY setup / compare MY quants" (local-bench's actual job); and (B) RELATIVE latent-ability models (Epoch Capabilities Index = 2PL IRT "Rosetta Stone", performance(m,b)=σ(α_b·[C_m−D_b]); LMArena Bradley-Terry/Elo; LLM-Stats TrueSkill on rank-order) — these RESIST saturation best and stitch benches of different difficulty onto one scale, but DRIFT when new models/items arrive, breaking "your score shouldn't move when someone else ships." For local-bench the verdict is: KEEP absolute chance-corrected normalization as the published per-setup score (methodology §3 is correct and survives), but ADOPT a 2PL/Rasch latent-ability layer (Epoch's exact functional form) as an INTERNAL discrimination diagnostic and saturation early-warning — exactly the §10 "1PL-before-2PL" roadmap, now externally validated by ECI shipping it at scale (40+ benches). Headline anti-saturation lever is item difficulty curation to 20-80% pass rate + reporting per-item discrimination (point-biserial / IRT α) and DROPPING/down-weighting saturated benches at a quarterly window — confirmed by the saturation meta-study (arXiv 2602.16763: 29/60 benches saturated, S_index=exp(−R_norm²); CRITICALLY it found PRIVATE test sets give NO saturation advantage, only LARGER sets + expert curation + dynamic refresh do — so local-bench's private-genmath sentinel is a CONTAMINATION canary, not an anti-saturation device, and the docs already frame it that way). CIs: the methodology's bootstrap-not-Wilson stance is correct and matches Epoch (n=500 bootstrap, ±1 SE, 16 reps/model) and LMArena (1000x bootstrap percentile CIs); for quant comparisons use PAIRED bootstrap / McNemar on item-level discordance (item noise cancels), never two independent CIs subtracted — already banked. Reasoning vs non-reasoning: keep strict LANES (never merge composites across lanes), report tokens-to-answer beside accuracy never folded in; AA's approach of just changing temperature (0 non-reasoning / 0.6 reasoning) within one index is the WRONG model for a quant-shopping audience.
key_takeaways: NO normalization is inherently saturation-proof. Saturation is a BENCHMARK-QUALITY problem solved by item selection (pick items models MISS, target 20-80% pass rate across the model range), reporting discrimination, and retiring/down-weighting saturated benches at a refresh window. The saturation meta-study (arXiv 2602.16763) found 29/60 benches at high/very-high saturation (S_index=exp(-R_norm^2)) and that LARGER expert-curated sets resist saturation while crowdsourced ones don't. This is the single most important lesson: local-bench's v0 failure (genmath 0.95-1.00 for everyone) is a content problem, not a math problem - no rescaling fixes a bench where the 9B scores 0.98.

TWO normalization families, opposite tradeoffs on the saturation-vs-drift axis. ABSOLUTE (chance-corrected mean / headroom buffer): Artificial Analysis averages pass@1 to 0-100 and clamps Elo components via clamp((Elo-500)/2000); LLM-Stats applies a 1.25 Headroom Buffer Factor mapping current SOTA to 80/100 to reserve 20% ceiling room. Temporally STABLE - your score is fixed once items are fixed. RELATIVE/latent (IRT, Elo, TrueSkill): Epoch ECI fits a 2PL IRT model performance(m,b)=sigma(alpha_b*[C_m - D_b]) across 40+ benches ('Rosetta Stone'), inferring each bench's difficulty D_b and saturation-slope alpha_b; LMArena fits Bradley-Terry; LLM-Stats uses TrueSkill on rank-order (scale-invariant, ranks thin-coverage models conservatively at mu-3*sigma). These RESIST saturation and unify different-difficulty benches but DRIFT as new models/items arrive.

FOR LOCAL-BENCH SPECIFICALLY: keep ABSOLUTE chance-corrected normalization as the PUBLISHED per-setup score (methodology section 3 is correct and survives the literature). Rationale: the product's job is 'where does MY model x quant sit and is MY quant ordering stable' - that demands temporal stability (Elo/IRT ability estimates re-rank everyone when a new model lands, violating the core promise) and absolute interpretability for the quant-delta wedge. Relative scores cannot answer 'Q4 cost you X points' in a way that survives a leaderboard reshuffle.

BUT ADOPT a 2PL/Rasch latent layer as an INTERNAL diagnostic (not the headline number). This is methodology section 10's '1PL-before-2PL' roadmap, now externally validated: Epoch ECI proves the exact functional form ships at scale, and the IRT compact-representation paper (arXiv 2510.00844) confirms 2PL with NO guessing parameter is the right LLM model, using item discrimination (alpha) to select items that separate strong from weak models. Use it for three things: (1) item-difficulty/discrimination estimates to CURATE the suite (drop low-alpha saturated items), (2) saturation early-warning per bench, (3) optional ability-estimate sanity check that local quant ordering is monotone. Do NOT publish IRT theta as the user-facing score (solo-founder trap: needs dense per-item response matrices + DIF checks across model families).

CONFIDENCE INTERVALS - the methodology is already correct and matches the field. Bootstrap, never Wilson (items aren't iid Bernoulli: shared subjects/templates/graders cluster failures -> reduced effective n). Cluster the bootstrap at the highest hierarchy level (resample QUESTIONS/strata, not sub-items). Field benchmarks: Epoch = bootstrap n=500, +/-1 SE bands, 16 reps/model on GPQA, SE kept to 2-3%; LMArena = 1000x bootstrap percentile (2.5-97.5%); AA claims +/-1% on the index from >10 reps. For QUANT comparisons use PAIRED bootstrap / McNemar on item-level discordance so item noise cancels - the 80%-power MDE drops to ~1.9pts paired vs ~13pts unpaired (already in the methodology's three-estimand table). Report repeatability CI and generalization CI as SEPARATE numbers - never let marketing language ('Q4 degrades knowledge by X%') outrun the paired CI.

COMBINING DIFFERENT SCALES/CHANCE-BASELINES HONESTLY: chance-correct EACH bench first (signed=(raw-c)/(1-c), c=0.10 for 4-of-10 MCQ wait MMLU-Pro is 10-option so c=0.10, 0 for free-form), do NOT clamp before aggregating (clamping at 0 biases floor scores upward - keep signed values through all math, clamp only for cosmetic display and render 'approx chance' when CI crosses 0). Aggregate by capability DOMAIN with explicit published weights, NOT by bench count. Use ARITHMETIC mean for the sortable scalar (interpretable) but it is fully compensatory and hides axis damage ([66,66,48] and [60,60,60] both =60) - so ALSO report per-domain delta AND worst-axis delta, and make the per-axis PROFILE the headline with the scalar as the sortable summary. Reject geometric mean (collapses on one near-zero axis). All of this is already banked in methodology sections 2/5 and is correct.

REASONING vs NON-REASONING: keep STRICT LANES - composite computed within a lane only, lanes never merge. Report tokens-to-answer / cost beside accuracy, never folded into the capability number. This BEATS Artificial Analysis's approach (one index, only difference is temperature 0 vs 0.6) because local-bench's audience is choosing a deployable local setup where a reasoning model burning 10x tokens at the same accuracy is a DIFFERENT product, not a footnote. Quant-sensitivity is its own per-axis figure, not blended into capability.

CONTAMINATION RESISTANCE - the effective levers, ranked by what the evidence supports: (1) generated/templated variants with a withheld private sentinel at matched difficulty (local-bench's genmath split) - public-vs-private gap is the GAMING/CONTAMINATION canary; (2) date-windowing / live refresh (LiveBench ships new questions monthly off post-cutoff arXiv/news/IMDb, keeps top models <70%) - the strongest demonstrated method (Codeforces pre/post-cutoff cliff); (3) canary GUID strings (BIG-Bench style) - WEAK alone (removable, needs logprobs, useless black-box). CRITICAL nuance the meta-study adds: a PRIVATE held-out set gives NO saturation resistance and only modest contamination benefit - its value is the differential signal (public-private gap), not secrecy per se. Methodology section 6 already frames the sentinel as a canary - keep that framing, do not oversell privacy as anti-saturation.
lessons_for_us: KEEP methodology section 3 (absolute chance-corrected normalization) as the published per-setup score - it is the correct choice for a 'track my setup / compare my quants' product and is validated by AA and LLM-Stats using absolute/headroom-buffered means. Do NOT switch the headline number to Elo/IRT/TrueSkill: those re-rank everyone when a new model ships, which breaks the product's temporal-stability promise.

ADD a headroom buffer to the display scale to pre-empt the next saturation cliff: borrow LLM-Stats' 1.25 factor idea - map current frontier-anchor SOTA to ~80/100 rather than 100, reserving ceiling room so a future better model has somewhere to go and so today's frontier doesn't visually pin the top. Apply at the COMPOSITE display layer only; keep raw chance-corrected values internally for the quant-delta math.

BUILD the 2PL/Rasch latent layer NOW but as an internal QA/curation tool, not the user number. Concretely: after each suite refresh, fit performance(m,b)=sigma(alpha_b*[C_m - D_b]) (Epoch's exact form) on the anchor+community response matrix; use per-ITEM discrimination (point-biserial as the cheap proxy, IRT alpha when data is dense enough) to PRUNE low-discrimination saturated items and to pick replacements that the frontier anchors actually miss. This operationalizes the section-3 'bench-quality gate' and the section-10 roadmap. tinyBenchmarks (Polo 2024) is the citable precedent for IRT-driven item subsetting.

SET an explicit per-bench RETIREMENT/down-weight rule keyed to a saturation index: compute S_index=exp(-R_norm^2) (or the simpler 'all frontier anchors within 1 point' rule the saturation meta-study and AA both use) at the quarterly window; any axis where the spread between the 9B-class local floor and the frontier ceiling collapses gets harder items injected or its weight cut. This is exactly what killed v0 genmath and must be a standing gate, not a one-off fix. Larger expert-curated item pools resist saturation; tiny generated sets at fixed difficulty do not.

DO NOT rely on the private genmath sentinel as a saturation defense - the meta-study found private sets give no saturation advantage. Re-scope it (already done in methodology section 6) purely as the contamination/gaming canary via the public-vs-private gap, and ADD a date-windowed / refreshable axis (LiveBench-style: regenerate templated items each quarter, or pull from post-training-cutoff sources) as the actual contamination-resistance workhorse.

PUBLISH the discrimination and saturation diagnostics on the methodology page, not just the scores. The saturation meta-study's headline recommendation is 'report uncertainty-aware statistics and compression indicators.' Showing per-axis discrimination + S_index + the anchor spread is a credibility MOAT vs AA/LLM-Stats (who largely don't) and directly answers the v0 critique - it makes 'does this bench actually separate models' visible to users.

LOCK the reasoning lanes and the tokens-beside-accuracy rule in code, and resist any future pressure to merge into one AA-style index. For a quant-shopping audience, efficiency (tokens/cost to reach an answer) is a first-class axis. Keep composite within-lane; show reasoning vs non-reasoning as a deliberate UI split.

GOVERNANCE: version index-v{n} = {normalization, domains, weights, aggregation, CI method, headroom factor, saturation/retirement rule} and suite-v{n} = items, independently. The field refreshes constantly (AA went v4.0 Jan 2026 after the old index capped at 73; LiveBench refreshes monthly). Local-bench needs the same disciplined, dated, never-silent versioning so a re-pruned suite doesn't silently move historical per-setup scores - pin each run to its suite-version.
benchmarks_used: Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.0.4 (10 evals, 4 categories x 25%; pass@1 averaged; GDPval Elo clamped via clamp((Elo-500)/2000); >10 reps for index CI <+/-1%)
Epoch Capabilities Index / 'Rosetta Stone' (2PL IRT over 40+ benchmarks; performance(m,b)=sigma(alpha_b*[C_m - D_b]); bootstrap n=500, +/-1 SE, 16 reps on GPQA Diamond)
LiveBench (monthly-refreshed, contamination-limited, objective ground-truth scoring, top models <70%)
LMArena / Chatbot Arena (Bradley-Terry MLE; 1000x bootstrap percentile 95% CIs)
LLM-Stats Score (TrueSkill on rank-order, scale-invariant; mu-3*sigma conservative ranking for thin coverage; 1.25 headroom buffer mapping SOTA->80)
tinyBenchmarks (Polo et al. 2024 - IRT-based item subsetting for sample-efficient eval)
MMLU-Pro (cited as a contamination-resistant successor; 10-option MCQ, chance=0.10) and IFEval (programmatic instruction-following) - local-bench's current axes
normalization_method: RECOMMENDED FOR LOCAL-BENCH (validated, mostly already in methodology v2): (1) Per-bench chance-correction signed=(raw-c)/(1-c) with c = the bench's true chance baseline (MMLU-Pro 0.10, IFEval/genmath 0); do NOT clamp before aggregating (clamping at 0 biases sub-chance floor scores upward) - carry signed values through all math, clamp to [0,1] for cosmetic display only and render 'approx chance' when the CI crosses 0. (2) Aggregate by capability DOMAIN (knowledge/reasoning, math, coding, agentic, instruction-following) with explicit published versioned weights, NOT by bench count; added benches split their domain's weight. (3) ARITHMETIC mean for the single sortable scalar (interpretable, but fully compensatory so ALSO report per-domain delta + worst-axis delta and lead the UI with the per-axis profile); reject geometric mean (collapses on a near-zero axis). (4) ADD a headroom buffer at the composite DISPLAY layer (LLM-Stats-style 1.25 factor mapping current frontier-anchor SOTA to ~80/100) to reserve ceiling room and defer the next saturation cliff. This is ABSOLUTE normalization, chosen over relative (Elo/IRT/TrueSkill ability scores) because temporal stability and absolute quant-delta interpretability are the product's core promises - relative scores re-rank everyone whenever a new model or item enters and cannot honestly express 'Q4 cost you X points on these items'. The relative/latent approaches (Epoch's 2PL IRT sigma(alpha_b*[C_m-D_b]), LMArena Bradley-Terry, LLM-Stats TrueSkill) are adopted ONLY as an internal saturation/discrimination diagnostic layer, never as the published per-setup number.
weighting_method: Equal-weight by capability DOMAIN, not by benchmark count (so a domain covered by 3 benches doesn't get 3x the influence of a single-bench domain) - matches Artificial Analysis (4 categories x 25%) and the methodology's existing knowledge/IF/math 1/3 split. Within a domain, benches split that domain's weight; a saturated bench gets its weight cut at the quarterly refresh window rather than silently distorting the axis. Weights are explicit, published on the methodology page, and versioned as part of index-v{n}; per-user slider override deferred. Avoid implicit weighting-by-coverage (the failure mode of TrueSkill/IRT pooled fits, where benches with more participating models produce larger updates) for the published number - keep weighting transparent and intentional. Critically, do NOT let a single discriminating axis get diluted: local-bench v0 failed partly because the only discriminating axis (MMLU-Pro) was 1/3 of a composite whose other 2/3 were saturated - weight should follow discrimination/information, with low-information saturated axes down-weighted.
handles_saturation_how: PRIMARY (content, not math): item-difficulty curation - select/generate items that target 20-80% pass rate ACROSS the local-to-frontier model range so models actually MISS them; the saturation meta-study (arXiv 2602.16763) confirms larger expert-curated pools resist saturation while small fixed-difficulty crowdsourced sets do not. Compute a saturation index per axis at a quarterly window (S_index=exp(-R_norm^2), or the operational 'all frontier anchors within ~1 point' rule used by AA and the meta-study) and DROP or down-weight any axis whose local-floor-to-frontier-ceiling spread has collapsed - a STANDING gate, since this is exactly what broke v0 genmath (0.95-1.00 for everyone incl. the 9B). SECONDARY (presentation): headroom buffer on the display scale (LLM-Stats 1.25, SOTA->80/100) so the frontier doesn't visually pin the ceiling and a future model has room. STRUCTURAL early-warning: the internal 2PL/Rasch layer surfaces per-item discrimination (point-biserial / IRT alpha); low-discrimination items are saturation in progress and get pruned (tinyBenchmarks precedent). Refresh cadence borrowed from LiveBench (monthly/quarterly new items off post-cutoff sources keeps top models <70%). Explicitly NOT relied upon: private holdouts (meta-study: no saturation advantage) and 'open-ended format' (meta-study: no systematic advantage). Note: relative scoring (Epoch ECI 2PL IRT, Elo) resists saturation best of all by stitching different-difficulty benches onto one latent scale - local-bench harvests this ONLY internally for diagnostics, accepting absolute normalization's saturation exposure in exchange for the temporal stability the product requires.
confidence_interval_treatment: Bootstrap, NOT Wilson/binomial - items are not iid Bernoulli (shared subjects, templates, graders, and IFEval multi-instruction items cluster failures, reducing effective n). Cluster the bootstrap at the highest hierarchy level: resample QUESTIONS/strata (within subject/template/difficulty buckets), not sub-items. This matches the field: Epoch = stratified bootstrap n=500 with +/-1 SE bands and 16 reps/model (SE held to 2-3% on GPQA); LMArena = 1000x bootstrap percentile (2.5-97.5%); AA claims index CI <+/-1% from >10 reps. THREE separately-reported estimands (already in methodology): (a) Repeatability CI (run-to-run only, same fixed items) ~ +/-1.0 at Quick-tier 252; (b) Paired quant-delta via PAIRED bootstrap / McNemar on item-level discordance - item noise cancels, 80%-power MDE ~1.9pts (vs ~13pts unpaired), reported as 'on local-bench suite-v{n} fixed items, -X.X +/- [paired CI]', NEVER subtracting two independent CIs and NEVER as a universal percentage; (c) Generalization CI (item-sampling, single-run ~ +/-6.5) reserved for Standard tier / pooled cross-user data. Composite CI = nested bootstrap (item resample (x) repeated-run stochasticity); do NOT combine asymmetric interval endpoints in quadrature. Display rule: when a CI crosses chance, render 'approx chance' rather than a misleadingly precise sub-chance point estimate. The honesty rule (marketing language may never outrun the paired CI) is the load-bearing constraint for the quant-delta product wedge.
reasoning_vs_nonreasoning_handling: STRICT LANES, never merged. Compute the composite WITHIN a reasoning lane only; a reasoning-enabled and a non-reasoning setup are never averaged into one capability number. Report tokens-to-answer (and cost/latency) BESIDE accuracy, never folded into the capability score - for a quant-shopping audience a model that matches accuracy at 10x token burn is a materially different product, not a footnote. Quant-sensitivity is its own per-axis figure, not blended into capability. This deliberately DIVERGES from Artificial Analysis, which runs one index and treats reasoning vs non-reasoning as merely a temperature setting (0 for non-reasoning, 0.6 for reasoning) - acceptable for ranking the frontier, wrong for local-bench's 'which deployable setup should I run' use case where efficiency is first-class. Sampling within a lane should follow each lab's recommended settings (greedy/temp 0 for non-reasoning; the model's recommended reasoning temperature and max-token budget for reasoning) to avoid penalizing reasoning models on truncation, mirroring AA's per-model-recommended-settings practice. The web UI should present reasoning vs non-reasoning as an explicit split, with the lane caveat shown on every leaderboard view (already in the prototype's 'no rank numbers for unranked Quick + lane caveat' honesty pattern).
critiques_of_field: Production composite leaderboards (Artificial Analysis, LLM-Stats) publish a headline scalar but thin methodology on cross-benchmark normalization and almost no per-axis DISCRIMINATION reporting - the exact blind spot that let local-bench v0 ship a non-discriminating composite; the saturation meta-study's top recommendation (report compression/discrimination indicators) is widely ignored in production.

Single-scalar composites are fully compensatory and hide axis-specific damage ([66,66,48] and [60,60,60] both average 60); most indices lead with the scalar and rarely surface worst-axis/per-domain deltas, which is what a quant-shopper actually needs.

Relative/Elo/IRT-ability leaderboards (LMArena, Epoch ECI, LLM-Stats TrueSkill) resist saturation but DRIFT - a model's number changes when other models/items enter the pool - and this temporal instability is rarely flagged as a limitation for 'track my own setup' use cases.

The field over-credits private held-out sets as a saturation defense; arXiv 2602.16763 shows private sets give NO saturation advantage and contamination benefit is mainly the differential public-vs-private gap, not secrecy.

Chance-baseline handling is frequently sloppy - averaging a 10-option MCQ (chance 0.10) with free-form (chance 0) on raw 0-100 without chance-correction inflates the high-chance axis and produces spurious model-order flips (the Gemini-beats-Opus-on-easy-axes artifact).
Clamping scores at 0 before aggregation biases sub-chance floor estimates upward, distorting exactly the small-model/degraded-quant region local-bench targets.
Answer-extraction fragility (MCQ regex mis-scoring 'A or B', terminal lists, 'A, B is wrong') is under-reported and can swamp real quant deltas if not hardened.
presentation_patterns: Lead with the per-axis PROFILE (radar/bar across knowledge/reasoning/math/coding/agentic/IF); make the single composite a SORTABLE summary column, not the hero number - directly answers the v0 critique and what quant-shoppers need.
Show confidence intervals on every score (error bars), and render 'approx chance' instead of a precise sub-chance point estimate when a CI crosses the chance baseline.

Quant deltas as PAIRED comparisons with dominance language (better / worse / tied / MIXED, each 'within uncertainty'), scoped 'on suite-v{n} fixed items', never as a universal percentage - the honesty wedge.
Publish the saturation/discrimination diagnostics (per-axis anchor spread, S_index, point-biserial/IRT discrimination) on the methodology page as a credibility moat vs AA/LLM-Stats.
Headroom-buffered display scale (SOTA-anchor -> ~80/100) so the frontier doesn't visually pin the ceiling and future improvement is visible.
Explicit reasoning vs non-reasoning lane split with a persistent lane caveat on every leaderboard view; tokens/cost shown beside accuracy.

Dated, versioned methodology: index-v{n} and suite-v{n} tags visible on each run so re-pruned suites don't silently move historical per-setup scores (AA dates v4.0.4; LiveBench dates monthly releases).

Conservative ranking for thin-coverage entries (LLM-Stats mu-3*sigma pattern): don't show a confident rank for a setup with few items/reps - the prototype's 'no rank numbers for unranked Quick' already does this.
Anchors shown as dashed reference lines on the quality-vs-VRAM scatter (already in the prototype) so users read their local setup against the frontier band, not as a head-to-head with API models.
